stem:
Match the following muscles with their action:
MuscleAction
a. Pectoralis major1. Extension at shoulder
b. Supraspinatus2. Flexion at shoulder
c. Infraspinatus3. Lateral rotation of the shoulder
d. Latissimus dorsi4. Abduction at shoulder
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
MuscleAction
a. Pectoralis major2. Flexion at shoulder
b. Supraspinatus4. Abduction at shoulder
c. Infraspinatus3. Lateral rotation of the shoulder
d. Latissimus dorsi1. Extension at shoulder
• Pectoralis major is a strong adductor, internal rotator of the humerus at the shoulder joint. It causes flexion at the shoulder.
• Supraspinatus is a rotator cuff muscle and is responsible for abduction at the shoulder.
• Infraspinatus is also a rotator cuff muscle responsible for the powerful lateral rotator of the humerus/shoulder.
• Latissimus dorsi is a superficial muscle of the back responsible for extension at the shoulder.

stem: Identify the type of joint between malleus and incus in the following image:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Articulating surfaces between malleus and incus are saddle shaped le. one surface is convex while the other is concave.
• Incus and stapes joint type is ball-and-socket synovial joint.
• Saddle synovial joint: Examples include:
1. Joint between malleus and incus.
2. Sternoclavicular joint.
3. 1st carpometacarpal joint
4. Calcaneocuboid joint.

Other Options:
Option A: • Examples: Glenoid fossa and head of humerus
• Ball and socket joints allow for a wide range of motion in multiple directions. The rounded head of one bone fits into the concave socket of another, facilitating movements like flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and rotation.
Option C: • Examples: Atlantoaxial joint (between the first and second cervical vertebrae)
• Description: Pivot joints allow rotation around a central axis. In the case of the atlantoaxial joint, the first cervical vertebra (atlas) rotates around the odontoid process of the second cervical vertebra (axis).
Option D: • Examples: Lateral end of clavicle and acromion process (acromioclavicular joint)
• Gliding joints (also known as plane joints) have flat surfaces that glide or slide past each other. They allow for limited movement in various directions, such as sliding or twisting.

stem: Identify the muscles supplied by the lateral plantar nerve:

Flexor digiti minimi brevis, Abductor digiti minimi, Adductor hallucis
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Muscles supplied by the lateral nerve:
• Abductor digiti minimi: Lateral plantar nerve (S2, S3) - Abducts and flexes the little toe.
• Quadratus Plantae: Lateral plantar nerve (S2, S3) - Flexes lateral four toes.
• Adductor hallucis: Lateral plantar nerve (S2, S3)-Adducts the big toe.
• Flexor digiti minimi brevis: Lateral plantar nerve (S2, S3) - Flexes proximal phalanx of the little toe.
• Plantar Interossel (3): Lateral plantar nerve (S2, S3) - Adducts the toes 2-4, Flex metatarsophalangeal joints.
• Dorsal Interossel (4): Lateral plantar nerve (S2, S3) - Abducts the toes 2-4, Flex metatarsophalangeal joints.

Option A: Medial plantar nerve supplies flexor digitorum brevis, abductor hallucis, flexor hallucis brevis.
Option B: Extensor digitorum brevis muscle is supplied by the lateral terminal branch of deep fibular (peroneal nerve).

stem: A 45 year old male recently got diagnosed with Nutcracker syndrome due to compression of a vein which drains into IVC. Identify the vein:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Left Renal Vein directly drains into the Inferior Vena Cava (IVC), This vein receives blood from the left kidney and left gonadal vein.


Other Options:
Option A: The left gonadal vein (left testicular vein in males or left ovarian vein in females) drains into the left renal vein, and then the blood flows into the IVC.
Option B: The left adrenal vein also drains into the left renal vein, which eventually connects to the IVC.
Option D: • Right ascending lumbar vein drains into the azygous vein.
• Azygous vein is on the right side.
• Azygous will drain into the Superior Vena Cava (SVC) at T4 level.

stem: A 54 year old man was brought to the emergency department by his wife, who described him as unresponsive to verbal commands. On examination at the emergency department, he was disoriented to time and place. Confusion with global aphasia was noted first and was later found to be pure word deafness with expressive and receptive aphasia. Which of the following sulcus will be injured in this a patient with trauma to the pointed area manifesting the above symptoms?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The marked part in the above image is the Pterion. The Pterion is a crucial anatomical landmark where several skull bones converge, forming an H-shaped sutural junction.
• It's particularly vulnerable due to the thinness of the bones in this region.
• The lateral sulcus, or Sylvian sulcus, divides the temporal lobe below from the parietal lobe above.
• The middle cerebral artery (MCA), a branch of the internal carotid artery (ICA), runs within it, supplying the lateral cerebrum.


Other Options:
Option A: The central sulcus runs anterior-inferiorly, dividing the frontal motor cortex from the parietal sensory cortex, but it's not near the pterion.
Option C: The inferior frontal sulcus is located on the frontal lobe anterior to the stem of the lateral sulcus and located close to the pterion but not in direct association with the pterion.
Option D: The intraparietal sulcus (IPS) runs along the lateral surface of the parietal lobe, with oblique and horizontal segments. It's not close to the pterion.

stem: Identify the structure marked in the given Image:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The marked structure in the given image is the pulmonary trunk.
• The pulmonary trunk starts from the upper part of the right ventricle, near the left 3rd costal cartilage, and runs backward and leftward.
• It ends beneath the aortic arch, in front of the left principal bronchus, dividing into the right and left pulmonary arteries.


Other Options:
Option A: Relation with Pulmonary Trunk: The left main bronchus passes beneath the aortic arch and behind the pulmonary trunk before entering the left lung, forming the bronchopulmonary angle.
Option B: Relation with Pulmonary Trunk: The pulmonary trunk is positioned anterior-left to the ascending aorta, which partially encircles its superior and posterior aspects as it extends Into the aortic arch.
Option C: Relation with Pulmonary Trunk: The pulmonary trunk is located anteriorly and to the left of the ascending aorta, which is continuous with the aortic arch.

stem: Identify the artery of the marked area number '10' in the given Image:

explanation:
Solution
• The highlighted structure is the inferior cerebellar peduncle, a white matter bundle linking the medulla oblongata to the cerebellum.
• It is mainly supplied by the Posterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery (PICA), a branch of the vertebral artery.


Other Options:

Option C: Superior cerebellar artery (SCA) supplies the superior surface of the cerebellum, superior and middle cerebellar peduncles, and the lower midbrain.
Option D: The Posterior Cerebral Artery (PCA) provides blood supply to the occipital cortex, inferior and medial temporal cortices, thalamus, and rostral midbrain.

stem: Identify the structure marked in the given Image:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Terminal bronchioles differ from larger bronchi as they are devoid of cartilage in their walls.
• The ciliated cells are still present in terminal bronchioles, but their abundance is reduced.
• Additionally, terminal bronchioles exhibit alveolar outgrowths, marking the transition to structures involved in gas exchange.


Other Options:
Option A: • Respiratory bronchioles have alveoli budding directly from their walls.
• This is a distinctive feature that indicates their role in gas exchange.
• Epithelium may become more cuboidal, eventually transitioning to simple squamous epithelium in the alveoli.
• Similar to terminal bronchioles, respiratory bronchioles lack cartilage in their walls.
Option C: • Alveolar sacs are composed of clusters of alveoll, which are small, balloon-like structures.
• Alveolar walls are extremely thin to facilitate efficient gas exchange.
Option D: • Intrapulmonary bronchi have smooth muscle in their walls, which helps regulate airflow.
• Goblet cells, found in the epithellum of Intrapulmonary bronchi, secrete mucus to aid in capturing particles and facilitating their expulsion from the respiratory tract.
• Cartilage found in the intrapulmonary bronchi helps maintain the shape of the bronchi, preserving the integrity of the airway structure.

stem: Which of the following structures is derived from the area marked in 'yellow' in the given Image?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Structures derived from the paraxial mesoderm include the somites.
Somites are bilaterally paired blocks of mesoderm that form on either side of the neural tube during early embryonic development. The somites give rise to various structures in the embryo, including the axial skeleton (such as vertebrae), skeletal muscles, and the dermis of the skin.


Other Options:
Option B: Kidneys are the derivatives of the intermediate mesoderm.
Option C: Dorsal root ganglia are derived from the neural crest cells.
Option D: The pericardial, pleural, and peritoneal cavities, housing vital organs, originate from the lateral plate mesoderm during embryonic development.

stem: The marked structure relays efferents to which nucleus of the thalamus?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The marked structure is the fornix. Fornix is the major efferent tract of the hippocampus.
The fornix sends efferents to the mammillary bodies, and the mammillothalamic tract connects the mammillary bodies to the anterior nucleus of the thalamus.
This pathway is part of the Papez circuit, involved in memory and emotional processing.

Other Options:
Option B: The lateral nucleus functions as the hunger center, promoting eating when stimulated, while its destruction leads to starvation.
Option C: The ventro-posterolateral nucleus of the thalamus processes sensations like pain, vibration, proprioception, and light touch from the spinothalamic tract and dorsal column pathways.
Option D: The posterior nucleus alds in thermoregulation by promoting heat conservation. It triggers the sympathetic nervous system, causing peripheral vasoconstriction to retain heat.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of blood in her stools for 2 weeks. Physical exam reveals firm, enlarged Inguinal lymph nodes. options: Anal canal Inferior to the pectinate line
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The lymph from the Inferior part of the anal canal drains to the superficial inguinal nodes.
The lymph from the distal rectum drains to the sacral nodes.
• The lymph from the sigmoid colon drains to the inferior mesenteric nodes and then to the internal Iliac nodes.
The lymph from the proximal rectum drains to the sacral nodes, and the lymph from the anal canal above the pectinate line drains to the internal iliac nodes.

Other Options:
Option B: This option is incorrect as lymph from the Inferior part of the anal canal drains to the superficial inguinal nodes.
Option C: This option is Incorrect as sigmoid colon lymph nodes drain to the inferior mesenteric nodes.
Option D: This option is Incorrect as proximal rectum drains to the sacral nodes.

stem: Active sites are clefts or grooves on the surface of an enzyme, usually composed of amino acids. Active site of an enzyme is mostly complementary to:
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
A substrate is a molecule that an enzyme reacts with.
The enzyme's active site, or the location where weak bonds between the two molecules can form, is loaded with a substrate.
An enzyme-substrate complex is formed, and the enzyme's pressures on the substrate drive it to react and become the planned reaction's result.
It is the most complementary to the active site.

Other Options:
Option A: Allosteric activators bind to locations on an enzyme away from the active site, inducing a conformational change that increases the affinity of the enzyme's active site(s) for its substrate(s).
Option C: The product is the molecule that is produced by the chemical reaction catalyzed by the enzyme.
In order for a product molecule to be generated, a substrate molecule must first bind to the active site of an enzyme and be transformed into a product molecule.
Option D: The transition state is the intermediary state of the reaction when the molecule is neither a substrate nor a product.
The transition state has the highest free energy, making it a rare and unstable intermediate.

stem: After 3 days of dengue infection, diagnosis can be made by all except:
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution

Other Options:

stem: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic condition that affects a protein in the body. What happens to chloride levels in sweat and pancreatic secretions in Cystic fibrosis?
options: Chloride concentration increases in pancreatic secretion
Chloride concentration normal in pancreatic secretion
subject: Pathology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
In cystic fibrosis (CF), the dysfunction of the CFTR protein leads to impaired chloride transport across cell membranes.
• The defective CFTR protein results in decreased chloride reabsorption by sweat glands, leading to higher chloride concentrations in sweat.
Sweat chloride levels above 60 mmol/L are Indicative of cystic fibrosis.

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: These are incorrect.

stem:
A cough is a reflex action to clear your airways of phlegm and irritants. Which among the following statements best describes the appropriate mechanism Involved in coughing?
1. Forceful expiration against a closed glottis
2. Expiration against an open glottis
3. Normal Inspiration
4. Removing harmful substances
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Forceful expiration against a closed glottis:
1. This describes the Initial phase of a cough. The cough reflex begins with a deep inhalation, followed by the closure of the glottis (the opening between the vocal cords).
2. The expiratory muscles then contract forcefully, Increasing the pressure in the chest. When the glottis suddenly opens. the built-up pressure is released, producing a forceful expulsion of air and any irritants in the airway.
• Removing harmful substances: Coughing serves to clear the respiratory tract of irritants, such as mucus, dust, smoke, or other harmful substances.
This is a protective reflex mechanism aimed at keeping the airways clear and preventing the aspiration of foreign materials.

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: are Incorrect.

stem: What is the primary mechanism by which parathyroid hormone (PTH) elevates blood calcium levels?
subject: Physiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Increased renal reabsorption of Calcium: PTH increases renal calcium reabsorption (distal tubule) and decreases phosphate reabsorption (proximal tubule).
Stimulation of osteoclast activity: PTH Indirectly increases the activity of osteoclasts. This releases calcium and phosphate from the bone turnover.
Stimulates Vitamin D synthesis: Increases absorption of calcium and phosphate in the small bowel by activating Vitarnin D.

Other Options:
Option A: Increased renal reabsorption of Calcium: This statement is true.
Option B: Stimulation of osteoclast activity: This statement is true.
Option C: Stimulates Vitamin D synthesis: This statement is true.

stem: The diagram displays nerve cell physiology. The event marked by 5 to 6 is due to
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
1 to 2: Stimulation of neurons leads to the generation of local potential towards the threshold voltage.
2 to 3: It is followed by a rapid increase in the membrane potential due to the influx of Na+ by the opening of the fast type of voltage-gated channel marked by the depolarization phase.
3 to 4: Next phase of the action potential is repolarization characterized by closing the voltage-gated Na+ channel and opening of the slow type of voltage-gated K+ channel leading to efflux of the K+.
4: The last phase is hyperpolarization, characterized by the presence of some already open K+ channels and K+ leak channels leading to continuous efflux of K+.

Other Options:
Options A, B and D: are incorrect.

stem: A patient was started on antitubercular therapy. After 12 days, Investigations were done. AST and ALT levels were within normal limits but serum creatinine was 0.8 mg/dL and serum uric acid was 10 mg/dL. What is the next best step in management?
options: Stop pyrazinamide and continue rest of the drugs
Add uric acid reducing drugs and continue ATT as such with LFT KFT monitoring
Continue all drugs and monitor serum uric acid level and LFT and KFT
Stop all ATT drugs till labs are normal
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Liver and renal functions of this patient are in the normal range, but serum uric acid is elevated.
For arthralgia, 3 ATT drugs can be responsible Pyrazinamide, FQ, and bedaquiline.
Management of arthralgia:
1. Initiate Paracetamol or NSAIDS.
2. Lower the dose of Pyrazinamide.
3. Discontinue the regimen if pain is severe.
• Points to note in arthralgia:
1. Symptoms of arthralgia generally diminish over time, even without intervention.
2. Uric acid level might be elevated.

Other Options:
Option A: Discontinuing pyrazinamide is not an appropriate answer since it compromises the ATT regimen. Mild elevations in uric acid levels with diffuse joint pain is not an indication to stop ATT and the patient can be managed with the measures listed above.
Option B: This option is incorrect because the patient has no specific symptoms of gout.
Option D: This option is Incorrect because ATT should not be discontinued for mild elevations in uric acid and joint pains.

stem: Which of the following drugs is used to prevent painful vaso-occlusive crises and prevent long-term complications in sickle cell anemia?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Hydroxyurea, a myelosuppressive agent, is the only effective drug proven to reduce the frequency of painful episodes.
It raises the level of HbF and the hemoglobin level.
It usually decreases the rate of painful episodes by 50%. Its mechanisms of action for HbF induction remain Incompletely understood.
Current evidence suggests that several potential mechanisms of action by hydroxyurea may be relevant for patients with SCA, which together lead not only to HbF induction but also to additional benefits.
The most important mechanism of action is inhibition of ribonucleotide reductase (RR), the enzyme involved in transforming ribonucleosides into deoxyribonucleosides that serve as building blocks for DNA synthesis.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect as this drug is of no use in preventing painful vaso-occlusive crises and preventing long-term complications in sickle cell anemia.
Option C: This option is incorrect as this drug is of no use in preventing painful vaso-occlusive crises and preventing long-term complications in sickle cell anemia.
Option D: This option is incorrect as this drug is of no use in preventing painful vaso-occlusive crises and preventing long-term complications in sickle cell anemia.

explanation:
Solution
SGLT-2 inhibitors inhibit reabsorption of Na+ and water back, hence Na and water goes out which is good in chronic heart disease.
Spironolactone
SGLT-2 inhibitors
ACE inhibitors
ARBS
ARNI (Combination of ARBs+sacubitril)
Beta Blockers
For African Americans - Hydralazine + Isosorbide dinitrate

Other Options:
Option B: Linagliptin is in a class of medications called dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors. It is of no use in chronic heart failure.
Option C: Rosiglitazone is a medication used in the management and treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus. It is in the thiazolidinediones class of medications. It is of no use in chronic heart failure.
Option D: This option is incorrect as it is of no use in chronic heart failure.

stem: A 57 seven year retired professor presented with exertional nocturnal dyspnoea, fatigue and cardiomegaly. Pedal oedema, gallop rhythm. On auscultation, there were rales in both the lungs. Which of the following drugs can be used to prevent the progression of the disease?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The likely diagnosis in this patient is heart fallure.
Captopril is an ACE Inhibitor that can be used to manage hypertension and heart fallure.
The following drugs can be used to prevent the progression of heart fallure:
1. Spironolactone
2. SGLT-2 inhibitors
3. ACE inhibitors
4. ARBS
5. ARNI (Combination of ARBs+sacubltril)
6. Beta Blockers
• The first drugs that a patient is started on as soon as a diagnosis of heart failure is made, are ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or ARNI (Angiotensin Receptor/Neprilysin Inhibitor).
There are many contraindications to beta-blockers, so the patient is usually started on a very low dose.

Other Options:
Option A: This is a calcium channel blocker that is absolutely contraindicated in CHF.
Option B: This option is incorrect as it has no role in decreasing mortality in heart failure. Digoxin only increases the force of contraction.
Option D: This option is incorrect as there are many contraindications to beta blockers and they are not considered first line..

stem: Which of the following is the disadvantage of oxcarbazepine over carbamazepine?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Adverse effects of carbamazepine are:
1. Hepatotoxicity
2. Dilutional hyponatremia
3. Steven-Jones syndrome
4. Agranulocytosis
5. Thrombocytopenia
6. Teratogenic
• Oxcarbazepine has fewer side effects but it has a higher incidence of dilutional hyponatremia in patients that take this drug.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is Incorrect because hepatotoxicity is more commonly seen with carbamazepine use.
Option B: This option is incorrect because Stevens-Johnson syndrome, although rare, is more commonly seen with carbamazepine use.
Option C: This option is incorrect because thrombocytopenia is more commonly seen with carbamazepine use.

stem: All of the following drugs are used in thyroid storm except:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Aspirin is of no use in thyroid storm. Aspirin displaces thyroxine from thyroxine-binding globulin and increases free T3 and T4 levels which can be dangerous and worsen thyroid storm.

Other Options:
Option A: Beta blockers are indicated to manage tachycardia in patients with thyroid storm.
Option B: Lugol's lodine inhibits further release of thyroxine.
Option C: It inhibits the conversion of T4 to T3.

stem: A patient presenting with purpura is diagnosed with Isolated thrombocytopenia. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor used in isolated thrombocytopenia is:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• It is a Tyrosine kinase inhibitor used in the management of chronic idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.
• The first line of drugs used to manage this condition is steroids.
Fostamatinib is only used in patients who do not respond to first-line therapy.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect because it is an IL-11 analog and it is used in thrombocytopenia induced due to anti-cancer drug therapy.
Option C: It is incorrect as it is a thrombopoletin receptor agonist used in Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.
Option D: It is incorrect as it is a thrombopoletin receptor agonist used in idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.

stem: Which is the incorrectly matched option in respect of the mechanism of action of the antimicrobial drugs?
options: Linezolid: Binds to 30S ribosome and inhibits protein synthesis
Rifampicin: Inhibits DNA dependent RNA polymerase
subject: Pharmacology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Protein synthesis inhibitors bind either to 30S or 50S.
• Drugs binding to 30S are - Tetracycline and aminoglycoside.
• Drugs binding to 50S are - Chloramphenicol, Linezolid, and clindamycin.

Other Options:
Option B: This option is correct as Rifampicin works by inhibiting DNA-dependent RNA polymerase.
Option C: This option is correct as Aztreonam works by inhibiting cell wall synthesis.
Option D: This option is correct as Daptomycin causes depolarization of membranes.

stem: Which drug shows intrinsic resistance?
options: Fluconazole - Candida glabrata and Candida krusel
subject: Pharmacology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Fluconazole does not work on Candida glabrata and Candida krusel.
• Resistance is mainly by efflux pump overexpression.
• ABC and MFS transporters.

Other Options:
Option B: This statement is incorrect as Amphotericin B is effective against Candida glabrata.
Option C: This statement is incorrect as Voriconazole is effective against Aspergillus fumigatus.
Option D: This option is incorrect as itraconazole is effective against Sporothrix.

stem: Patient was taking ampicillin. Now she is experiencing diarrhea. Drug of choice to manage this condition:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Antibiotics usually kill normal gut flora leading to multiplication of pathogenic bacteria (one of the most common among them is Clostridium difficile, which further causes pseudomembranous colitis; risk of it is also increased by usage of ampicillin, clindamycin, and 3rd generation cephalosporins).
• Drug of choice for this condition is Oral Fidaxomicin.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect as Oral metronidazole is not the drug of choice for this condition. It is the 2nd drug of choice.
Option C: This option is incorrect as Oral ciprofloxacin is not the drug of choice for this condition.
Option D: This option is incorrect as Bezlotoxumab is not the drug of choice for this condition.

stem: A young 20 year old patient on treatment with drugs for MDR tuberculosis presents with weight gain, cold Intolerance, and lethargy. On investigations, there were high levels of TSH and low levels of T3 and T4 in the blood. Which of the following is the Ilkely drug responsible for these symptoms?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The given clinical picture points towards hypothyroidism; the drug responsible in this scenario is Ethionamide.
The mechanism of ethionamide-induced hypothyroidism is potentially caused by the structure of ethionamide compounds being chemically similar to thiocarbamides, such as propylthiouracil (C_7H_10N_2S), which Inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is Incorrect as cycloserine causes psychosis and seizures.
Option C: This option is incorrect as it is ototoxic and is a teratogenic drug.
Option D: This option is Incorrect as its side effect is arthralgia and hyperuricemia.

stem: Which of the following protease inhibitors can cause altered mental status, headache, and intracranial bleed?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Tipranavir is a protease inhibitor that can cause altered mental status, headache, and intracranial bleeding.
• It increases the risk of bleeding; it is a rare complication.
• It can also cause hepatotoxicity.

Other Options:
Option A: It is incorrect as Tenofovir only decreases GFR and decreases bone mineral density.
Option B: This option is incorrect as it does not affect blood vessels.
Option D: This option is incorrect as it is not related to the above clinical picture.

stem: Which chelation therapy is used to prevent Doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Dexrazoxane is a medication used in the management and treatment of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity and extravasation Injuries.

Other Options:
Option A: It is incorrect as it is a neurokinin 1 blocker used for late CINV (Chemotherapy Induced Nausea and Vomiting).
Option B: It is incorrect as it is an iron chelator.
Option C: It is incorrect as it is used to prevent cisplatin nephrotoxicity.

stem: Which of the following acts by the following MOA?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Superantigens, in contrast to conventional antigens, don't require processing and presentation by antigen-presenting cells (APCs) to activate T cells.
Instead, they Interact laterally with the variable beta (VB) part of the T-cell receptor (TCR) outside the peptide-binding groove.


Other Options:
Option A: Cholera toxin is produced by Vibrio cholerae, the bacterium responsible for cholera. The toxin acts by ADP- ribosylation of the Gs protein in intestinal cells. This leads to increased intracellular CAMP levels, resulting in the continuous activation of the CFTR chloride channel and the efflux of chloride ions into the intestinal lumen. The osmotic imbalance causes massive diarrhea, leading to dehydration and potentially severe electrolyte imbalances.
Option C: Clostridium difficile is a bacterium that can cause antibiotic-associated diarrhea and colitis. It produces toxins, particularly toxin A (enterotoxin) and toxin B (cytotoxin). The toxins produced by Clostridium difficile rapidly target and attack the GTP-binding proteins, specifically Rho, Rac, and CDC 42. This attack results in considerable damage to the actin cytoskeleton, ultimately leading to cellular death.
Option D: Clostridium botulinum produces botulinum toxin, which is one of the most potent toxins known. Subtypes A, B and E cause infections, with A being the most severe. The toxin induces flaccid paralysis by blocking acetylcholine release, targeting Snare proteins. In neurons, it cleaves Snap 25, Syntaxin 1, and Synaptobrevin, preventing muscle contraction and causing floppy muscles. Clinical manifestations include the absence of muscle contraction, leading to flaccidity.

stem: A 47 year old farmer presents to the outpatient department with complaints of fever. On physical examination, this eschar is seen at the bite site. The patient's serum creatinine was 1.6 and platelets were 88000, What is your diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
The clinical presentation of a farmer with fever, thrombocytopenia (platelet count of 88.000), elevated serum creatinine levels (1.4), and the presence of a black eschar is suggestive of a diagnosis of scrub typhus.
Mite larvae → Seek the most area of body (axilla, groin) → After 1-3 weeks → Eschars (black lesion), Fever, Nausea, Vomiting → Complication: Pneumonia, ARDS, Shock


Other Options:
Option B: Anthrax usually presents with fever, malaise, and characteristic skin lesions (cutaneous anthrax). Thrombocytopenia and elevated creatinine levels are less commonly associated with anthrax
Option C: Leptospirosis, also known as "Well's disease," usually presents with fever, headache, muscle aches, and jaundice.. • Cutaneous black eschar is not seen in leptospirosis. Infection is likely to occur among rice paddy field workers who are exposed to water contaminated with rat urine, especially during rainy conditions.
Option D: Brucellosis is a zoonotic disease, potentially transmitted through direct contact with infected animals or the consumption of raw milk The disease is characterized by hepatosplenomegaly, attributed to the invasion of reticuloendothelial cells, particularly macrophages. Symptoms include night sweats, arthritis, and a low platelet count. The Incubation period typically spans from 1 week to 1 month. The black eschar is not seen with brucellosis.

stem: Which of the following diseases is not transmitted by the vector shown below?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Transmission: Scrub typhus, caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi infection, is conveyed through the bite of chigger larvae belonging to the Trombiculidae mite family, rather than hard ticks.

Other Options:
Option B: Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic Fever is primarily transmitted by ticks, including hard ticks.
Option C: Babesiosis is mainly transmitted by Ixodes ticks, which are hard ticks.
Option D: Ehrlichiosis can be transmitted by different types of ticks, including hard ticks such as Amblyomma and Dermacentor.

stem: A 33 year old sexually active male patient came to the OPD with complaints of pain in the genital area. On examination, there was a painful vesicle associated with painful lymphadenopathy. What is your diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Presentation: Painful vesicles in the genital area are characteristic of genital herpes. The virus can cause painful lymphadenopathy in the affected region.
• Clinical Features: Recurrent episodes of genital lesions, and flu-like symptoms during initial infection.
• Diagnosis: Often diagnosed through clinical presentation and confirmed by laboratory tests, including PCR and serology.

Other Options:
Option B: • Presentation: Molluscum contagiosum typically presents as small, painless, raised lesions with a central dimple.
• Clinical Features: Flesh-colored or pearly papules.
• Diagnosis: Clinical examination; a blopsy may be performed in certain cases.
Option C: • Presentation: Genital warts are associated with certain strains of HPV. They may be painless or cause minimal discomfort.
• Clinical Features: Warts in the genital or anal area.
• Diagnosis: Clinical examination; HPV testing in certain cases.
Option D: • Presentation: Herpes zoster (shingles) typically presents as a painful vesicular rash along a dermatome, not limited to the genital area.
• Clinical Features: Unilateral rash with grouped vesicles.
• Diagnosis: Clinical examination; laboratory tests like PCR.

stem: A 42 year old person from a rural area experiencing CNS symptoms and vision loss for the past 12 days, the electron microscopic examination reveals the presence of which organism?
subject: Microbiology, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• Acanthamoeba causes Granulomatous Amoebic Encephalitis (GAE), which has a subacute to chronic course (days to weeks).
• It is commonly seen in Immunocompromised Individuals, but can also affect healthy persons.
• Associated with CNS symptoms along with ocular Involvement (keratitis vision loss).
• Found in soll and contaminated water, especially in rural environments.
• Electron microscopy shows trophozoites with spine-like projections (acanthopodia), characteristic of Acanthamoeba.

Other Options:
Option A: - Naegleria fowleri
• Causes Primary Amoebic Meningoencephalitis (PAM).
• Disease is acute and rapidly fatal (within 2-5 days).
• Typically associated with freshwater exposure (swimming).
• Does not cause vision loss or chronic course, hence incorrect.
Option C: - Balamuthia
• Causes Granulomatous amoebic encephalitis, similar to Acanthamoeba.
• However, ocular Involvement is less typical
• Less commonly associated with vision loss, making Acanthamoeba more likely.
Option D: - None of the above
• A suitable organism (Acanthamoeba) is clearly listed.
• Hence, this option is Incorrect.

stem: Vibrio cholerae is a gram negative, comma shaped bacterium causing cholera. Vibrio cholerae disrupts which extracellular cell adhesion?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Function: Occludens refers to tight junction proteins (occludin, claudins) involved in forming tight junctions between epithelial cells.
• Role in Cholera: Vibrio cholerae secretes cholera toxin, which disrupts the tight junctions (occludens) in the intestinal. epithelium. This disruption leads to increased secretion of water and electrolytes, a characteristic feature of cholera.

Other Options:
Option A: • Function: Integrins are cell adhesion receptors involved in cell-to-cell and cell-to-extracellular matrix interactions.
• Role in Cholera: Vibrio cholerae does not primarily disrupt integrin-mediated adhesion.
Option B: • Function: Cadherins are proteins involved in calcium-dependent cell adhesion.
• Role In Cholera: While cadherins play essential roles in various cellular adhesion processes, they are not the primary targets of disruption by Vibrio cholerae.

stem: Which of the following viruses does need reverse transcription for PCR?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus):
• Genome Type: RSV has a negative-sense single-stranded RNA genome.
• Need for Reverse Transcription: Reverse transcription is required to convert the RNA genome into complementary DNA (cDNA) before PCR amplification.

Other Options:
Option B: EBV (Epstein-Barr Virus)
• Genome Type: EBV has a double-stranded DNA genome.
• Need for Reverse Transcription: Reverse transcription is not required for DNA viruses like EBV. PCR can directly amplify DNA targets.
Option C: CMV (Cytomegalovirus)
• Genome Type: CMV has a double-stranded DNA genome.
• Need for Reverse Transcription: Reverse transcription is not required for DNA viruses like CMV. PCR can directly amplify DNA targets.
Option D: Adenovirus
• Genome Type: Adenovirus has a double-stranded DNA, genome.
• Need for Reverse Transcription: Reverse transcription is not required for DNA viruses like Adenovirus. PCR can directly amplify DNA targets.

stem: A 23 year old male patient presented with a 3 day history of fever. CBC showed a platelet count of <90,000. For a suspicion of dengue which of the following cannot be done on day 3 of Infection?
options: Xenodiagnosis using culture in Aedes mosquito
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• IgM antibodies start to appear a few days after the onset of symptoms, making IgM ELISA less sensitive in the very early stages of infection.
• It might not be as reliable on day 3 but can become more useful after 6 days.

Other Options:
Option A: PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction): PCR is often most effective during the early viral phase of the infection when viral RNA is present in the bloodstream. Therefore, PCR is feasible and can be performed on day 3 to detect the viral genetic material.
Option B: NS1 Antigen Test: The NS1 antigen test detects a viral protein present in the serum of infected individuals. It is usually reliable from the early days of infection, making it a suitable option on day 3.
Option D: Xenodiagnosis using Culture In Aedes Mosquito: Xenodiagnosis Involves allowing Aedes mosquitoes to feed on the patient's blood and then testing the mosquitoes for the presence of the virus. This technique is generally not performed, but can be done in the early stage of infection.

stem: HIV is a virus that attacks the body's immune system. HIV genome needs which of the following to enter the membrane?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
The HIV genome requires the collaborative action of various components, Including GP120, CCR5 or CXCR4 co-receptors, and GP41, for entry into the host cell membrane. The initiation of this complex process is triggered by the binding of GP120 to the CD4 receptor on the host cell surface.
• Function: GP120 is a glycoprotein on the surface of the HIV envelope.
• Role In Entry: GP120 binds to CD4 receptors on the surface of host cells (usually CD4+ T cells), initiating the attachment of the virus to the cell membrane. This interaction exposes a binding site for a co-receptor.


Other Options:
Option B: CCR5 (Chemokine Receptor 5)
• Function: CCR5 is a chemokine receptor found on the surface of certain immune cells, including CD4+ T cells.
• Role In Entry: After GP120 binds to the CD4 receptor, it undergoes a conformational change, allowing it to interact with a co-receptor. CCR5 is a primary co-receptor used by some strains of HIV for membrane fusion and entry into the host cell.
Option C: GP41 (Glycoprotein 41)
• Function: GP41 is another glycoprotein on the HIV envelope.
• Role in Entry: GP41 facilitates the fusion of the viral envelope with the host cell membrane. It forms a hairpin-like structure (six-helix bundle) during this process, bringing the viral and cellular membranes in close proximity.
Option D: CXCR4 (Chemokine Receptor 4)
• Function: CXCR4 is another chemokine receptor found on the surface of immune cells.
• Role In Entry: Some strains of HIV can use CXCR4 as an alternative co-receptor to CCR5 during the entry process.

stem: A 35 year old female patient recovered from COVID-19, given steroids, 3 weeks complaints of nasal discharge, nasal image of fungus?
subject: Microbiology, ENT
explanation:
Solution
• In this clinical context, both COVID-19 and the administration of immunosuppressive agents, particularly steroids, can weaken the immune system.
• The accompanying image depicts fungal characteristics, including hyphae with aseptate morphology and branching angles exceeding or equal to 90 degrees. Additionally, the hyphae exhibit a broad ribbon-like shape. This is suggestive of the presence of mucor.

Other Options:
Option B: Aspergillus: Aspergillus typically has septate hyphae with acute-angle branching. The branching angles are not broad, and the hyphae are divided by septa.


Option C: Candida: Candida is a yeast that may form pseudohyphae and true hyphae, but it does not typically present with aseptate hyphae. Candida hyphae usually have septa.


Option D: Trichosporon: Trichosporon species may show septate hyphae. However, the characteristic branching angles of Trichosporon hyphae are not typically broad, as seen in Mucor.

stem: Which bacterial toxin acts by interfering with EF-2 function?
subject: Microbiology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Produced by Corynebacterium diphtheriae, the bacterium that causes diphtheria.
• Mechanism of Action: Diphtheria toxin inhibits protein synthesis by ADP-ribosylation of EF-2 This modification prevents EF-2 from participating in the translocation step during protein synthesis, leading to the inhibition of elongation and ultimately causing cell death.

Other Options:
Option B: Clostridium Toxins (Botulinum and Tetanus Toxins):
• Clostridium botulinum toxin and Clostridium tetani toxin act on different targets.
• Botulinum toxin: Inhibits the release of acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions, leading to muscle paralysis.
• Tetanus toxin: Interferes with neurotransmitter release, causing spastic paralysis.
Option C: Bacillus anthracis Toxin
• Bacillus anthracis produces a toxin known as anthrax toxin.
• Anthrax toxin does not directly interfere with EF-2 function but exerts its effects through the disruption of cellular signaling pathways.
Option D: Vibrio cholerae Toxin (Cholera Toxin)
• Cholera toxin primarily affects the regulation of ion transport in host cells by increasing intracellular levels of cyclic AMP (CAMP).
• It does not directly interfere with EF-2 function.

stem:
Which of the following is/are an advantage (s) of a saline mount compared to an lodine mount?
1. Motility of trophozoites
2. Internal structure of cysts
3. Differentiate bile stained vs non bile stained
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
1. Motility of Trophozoltes:
• Advantage of Saline Mount: Saline mounts are suitable for observing the motility of trophozoites (the active, motile form of some protozoa). In lodine mount trophozoites are immobilized.
2. Internal Structure of Cysts:
• Advantage of lodine Mount: lodine mounts are often preferred for the examination of internal structures, including the nuclear and cytoplasmic details of cysts. lodine enhances the contrast and visibility of internal features.
3. Differentiation Between Bile-Stained and Non-Bile-Stained:
• Advantage of Saline Mount: In iodine mounts, both bile-stained and non-bile-stained helminthic ova are stained brown.

stem: Identify the organism and staining:
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Nocardia is a Gram-positive bacterium that appears purple after Gram staining, forms filaments, and is detectable using the Ziehl-Neelsen staining method.

Other Options:
Option B: Streptococcus gram stain
• Streptococcus bacteria stain purple in the Gram staining method, Indicating their Gram-positive nature.
• Streptococcus bacteria typically have a spherical or ovold shape and can form chains or pairs. Unlike Nocardia, they do not display filamentous structures.
Option C: Cryptococcus India ink:
• Cryptococcus yeast cells are round to oval and may be encapsulated. The capsule is the key feature that will be visible in the India ink preparation, creating a halo around the stained cells. Unlike Nocardia, they do not display filamentous structures.
Option D: Coccidioldomycosis PAS stain
• Coccidioides species will appear magenta or pink due to the PAS stain, and characteristic structures like spherules may be visible. Unlike Nocardia, they do not display filamentous structures.

stem: A 55 year old HIV patient with white patch on the tongue or buccal mucosa which cannot be removed on rubbing. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Microbiology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
Oral Hairy Leukoplakia (OHL): This condition is strongly associated with EBV, and it presents as white, corrugated, or hairy-appearing patches on the lateral borders of the tongue or other oral mucosal surfaces. The white patches in OHL cannot be scraped off easily and are often asymptomatic.
Other Options:
Option B: CANDIDA
• Candidiasis: Candida infections in the oral cavity can cause white patches, commonly referred to as oral thrush. However, these patches can usually be rubbed off, revealing an erythematous (red) base. In the case described, where the white patch cannot be removed by scraping, candidiasis seems less likely.


Option C: HSV (Herpes Simplex Virus):
• Herpetic Ulcers: HSV infections can cause painful ulcers in the oral cavity, but they are not typically described as white patches that cannot be removed by scraping. HSV ulcers often have a yellowish base surrounded by an erythematous halo.
Option D: HHV8 (Human Herpesvirus 8):
Kaposi's Sarcoma: HHV8 is associated with Kaposi's sarcoma, which can present with lesions in the oral cavity. However, these lesions are more commonly reddish or purple rather than white, and they may not exactly fit the description given.

stem: Which organism shows movement transcellular by ACTIN FILAMENTS?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Listeria monocytogenes is a bacterium that can move within and between cells by exploiting the host cell's actin cytoskeleton.
It uses host cell machinery to polymerize actin filaments at one end of the bacterium, pushing it into the cell membrane. This allows Listeria to move within the host cell and from cell to cell.

Other Options:
Option B: EIEC (Enteroinvasive Escherichia coll): EIEC does invade and multiply within the epithelial cells of the colon, but its mechanism of movement is not primarily based on actin polymerization like Listeria.
Option C: Salmonella: Salmonella also invades host cells, but it uses a different mechanism involving a type III secretion system. It Induces rearrangement of the host cell cytoskeleton, but its movement is not primarily transcellular by actin filaments.

stem: A 18 days old baby was brought to the OPD with fever and cough and has vesicles over trunk and upper limb. Mother had genital ulcers 1 yr back but they have resolved now. The biopsy of vesicles shows multinucleated giant cells. The diagnosis is:
subject: Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The history of the mother having had genital ulcers in the past is significant, as the transmission of HSV to the baby can occur during childbirth if the mother is shedding the virus.
The presence of vesicles and multinucleated glant cells on biopsy further supports the diagnosis of HSV infection in the newborn.

Other Options:
Option B: Molluscum Contagiosum (MCV)
• Molluscum contagiosum is a viral skin infection caused by the Molluscipoxvirus.
• It typically presents as small, flesh-colored, dome-shaped papules with a central dimple.
• It is not associated with fever, cough, or multinucleated giant cells on biopsy.
Option C: Human Papillomavirus (HPV)
HPV is a DNA virus that causes warts. Genital warts are associated with certain strains of HPV.
While HPV can cause skin lesions, it doesn't typically present with systemic symptoms like fever and cough.
• The biopsy findings of multinucleated giant cells are not characteristic of HPV.
Option D: Varicella (Chickenpox)
• Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) causes chickenpox, characterized by a generalized vesicular rash.
• Chickenpox can occur in infants, but at 18 days old, the timing seems less likely for typical chickenpox.
• The multinucleated giant cells on biopsy are more suggestive of herpes simplex virus.

stem: Which of the following has the least teratogenicity?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pediatrics, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
HSV-2 has a low risk of congenital Infection because the transmission of herpes simplex virus to the newborn occurs most commonly (85%) during transvaginal birth in a woman with an active lesion of herpes in the genital tract of the mother. This is prevented by preferring a cesarean section for delivery of the baby and reducing the risk of transmission to the baby.
The transplacental route of transmission is uncommon (5%); thus, it is least teratogenic for the baby.
• If a mother acquires primary infection in the last trimester, then the baby may present with IUGR.

Other Options:
Option A: CMV: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) has a high risk of congenital infections and is transmitted transplacentally resulting in microcephaly along with periventricular calcification.
Option B: Chickenpox: Chickenpox is transmitted transplacentally and can result in congenital varicella syndrome characterized by hypoplasia of the limbs, IUGR, and microcephaly.
Option D: Rubella: The transplacental spread of the rubella virus results in congenital rubella syndrome characterized by patent ductus arteriosus, cataract, and SNHL (sensorineural hearing loss).

stem: A 17 year old girl manifested with fever and retro-orbital headache for 2 days. Tourniquet test and NS1 Ag were positive. Which of the following come under severe criteria?
subject: Medicine, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Retro-orbital headache and Tourniquet test is a typical point towards dengue.
NS1 Ag is positive and confirms the diagnosis of dengue.
• AST and ALT >1000 come under severe criteria in dengue.
• Dengue virus can directly affect the liver, leading to hepatic injury. The virus may replicate in the liver cells, causing inflammation and damage. This liver involvement is known as dengue hepatitis.
The liver may also be affected by the reduced blood supply, contributing to elevated liver enzymes.

Other Options:
Option A: Leucopenia plus thrombocytopenia: It is incorrect as leucopenia plus thrombocytopenia comes under probable dengue.
Option B: Persistent vomiting: This option is incorrect as persistent vomiting is a warning sign of dengue.
Option C: Hepatomegaly >1 cm: This option is incorrect as hepatomegaly >1 cm is a warning sign of dengue.

stem: Name the anticoagulant used in the light blue-colored vacutainer for measuring plasma glucose levels?
subject: Pathology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
The anticoagulant used in a light blue vacutainer is 3.2% trisodium citrate.

Option D: EDTA: EDTA is the anticoagulant used in vacutainers with a lavender top, pink top, white top, and tan top. These vacutainers contain EDTA in a powder form. White Purple lavender CBC PS Retlc count ESR (Wemobe TAN Plasma to PCR blum Thách element OUZIN P CA Mirtch

stem:
Match the columns:

stem: After class switching, a particular class of antibody is produced, but IgM and IgD are expressed concomitantly on the surface of a B cell due to which of the following phenomena?
subject: Pathology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Alternate RNA splicing:
• The co-expression of IgM and IgD after class switching is due to alternate RNA splicing.
• The primary reason IgM and IgD are co-expressed on the surface of B cells after class switching is that class switching Involves the recombination of constant region genes, but it does not alter the variable region of the antibody.
• The variable region is responsible for antigen specificity, and it remains the same even after class switching.

Other Options:
Option A: Allelic exclusion:
• Allelic exclusion is a mechanism that ensures that a B cell expresses antibodies derived from only one of its two parental alleles.
• It helps maintain the specificity of the B cell receptor on a single B cell.
Option C: Somatic hypermutation:
• Somatic hypermutation is a process that introduces random mutations into the variable regions of immunoglobulin genes.
• This mechanism Increases the diversity of antibodies during an Immune response.
Option D: Affinity maturation: Affinity maturation is a process during which B cells with higher-affinity receptors for an antigen are selected and survive, leading to an improved antibody-antigen interaction.

stem: A known case of sickle cell anemia has visited the clinic for regular check up. Which of the following for Sickle cell anemia is correct?

High mechanical fragility, low osmotic fragility
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
High mechanical fragility, Low osmotic fragility:
1. High mechanical fragility:
• Sickle cells have Increased mechanical fragility, meaning they are more prone to rupture or break under physical stress.
• The abnormal shape of sickle cells, which is rigid and less deformable than normal red blood cells, contributes to their increased mechanical fragility.
2. Low osmotic fragility:
• Sickle cells have decreased osmotic fragility, meaning they are less prone to rupture in a hypotonic (lower concentration of solutes) environment.
• The abnormal membrane permeability of sickle cells is less than originally stated, contributing to their decreased osmotic fragility.

Other Options:
Option A, B and C: are incorrect.

stem: Longitudinal, observational and analytical study is also known as?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
• The combination of a longitudinal, observational, and analytical study is known as a Cohort Study.
• Design: Prospective study following a group of individuals with a common characteristic or exposure over time.
• Longitudinal study is a type of cohort study where outcomes are determined at fixed time Intervals.
• A Cohort Study is an observational analytical study that provides evidence supporting the existence of an association between exposure and outcome.


Other Options:
Option A: Case-Control Study
• Design: Retrospective study comparing individuals with a specific outcome (cases) to those without the outcome (controls).
• Observational: Yes, it is observational, but it is not longitudinal.
Option C: Ecological Study
• Design: Examines the correlation between variables at the group level rather than the individual level.
• Observational: Yes, it is observational, but it is not longitudinal.
Option D: Randomized Control Trial (RCT)
• Design: Experimental study where participants are randomly assigned to different groups (treatment and control).
• Longitudinal: Yes, it is longitudinal as it Involves follow-up over time.
• Observational: No, it is an experimental design involving interventions.
• Analytical: Yes, it is analytical, but it involves experimental manipulation.

Option C: Multiple Regression Model
• A multiple regression model is a statistical tool used for analyzing the relationship between multiple variables.
• It is more associated with quantitative data analysis and statistical modeling than with visual representation in the form of graphs.

stem: In a scenario where a girl using DMPA (depot medroxyprogesterone acetate) experiences menstrual bleeding on the anticipated date and then again on the 15th day of her current cycle, what would be the recommended next step in management?
options: Perform a urinary pregnancy test (UPT) before administering DMPA
Administer DMPA along with 7 days of barrier contraception
subject: Obstetrics and Gynaecology, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• DMPA (Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate) commonly causes Irregular bleeding, especially in the Initial months of use.
• Breakthrough bleeding or Intermenstrual spotting (like day 15 bleeding) is a well-known side effect.
• If the patient has been receiving Injections on schedule (every 3 months), contraceptive protection is maintained.
• There is no need for additional contraception or delay if compliance is confirmed.
• Therefore, the correct step is to continue DMPA as per schedule without interruption.

Other Options:
Option A: - Urinary pregnancy test (UPT)
• A UPT is not routinely required if injections are taken regularly and on time.
• Irregular bleeding alone is not an indication of pregnancy in DMPA users.
• Testing is only needed if there is missed dose or pregnancy suspicion.
Option C: - DMPA with barrier contraception
• Barrier contraception is recommended only when there is a delay In Injection (>2 weeks).
• In this case, there is no missed or delayed dose mentioned.
• Adding contraception unnecessarily does not Improve efficacy.
Option D: - Wait until next period
• Waiting is not required and may lead to missed scheduled dosing.
• DMPA is given at fixed Intervals, not dependent on menstrual cycle timing.
• Delaying may Increase risk of contraceptive fallure.

stem: In a BSL-3 (Biosafety Level 3) laboratory, which of the following pathogens would be appropriately handled?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
XDR-TB is a highly drug-resistant form of tuberculosis. It requires handling in a BSL-3 laboratory due to its potential for airborne transmission and the need for stringent safety measures to protect laboratory workers.


Other Options:
Option B: Salmonella: Salmonella is typically handled at a lower biosafety level, BSL-2, as it is generally considered less dangerous than some other pathogens. Standard microbiological practices and precautions are usually sufficient.
Option C: Ebola: Ebola virus, causing a severe and often fatal illness, requires BSL-4 containment due to its high level of pathogenicity and the risk of aerosol transmission. BSL-4 laboratories have the highest level of containment.
Option D: Crimean-Congo Fever:
• Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic Fever virus is caused by a highly pathogenic Nairovirus in the Bunyaviridae family.
• Transmitted primarily by ticks, it poses a risk of human-to-human transmission through bodily fluids. CCHF is an emerging disease.
• Its severe clinical presentation includes hemorrhagic fever symptoms, and due to its high pathogenicity, the virus is handled in Biosafety Level 4 (BSL-4) laboratories, ensuring maximal containment and safety measures.

stem: Which statement accurately distinguishes between palliative care and hospice care?
options: Palliative care is primarily provided in hospitals, while hospice care is exclusively delivered in specialized hospice facilities

Palliative care focuses on providing comfort and relief of symptoms for patients with serious illnesses, regardless of prognosis, whereas hospice care is specifically tailored for patients with a prognosis of six months or less to live.

Palliative care is limited to end-of-life care, while hospice care addresses the physical, emotional, and spiritual needs of patients throughout their illness trajectory, including curative treatments.
Palliative care emphasizes aggressive treatment to prolong life, whereas hospice care prioritizes comfort and quality of life by foregoing curative treatments when appropriate.
subject: Medicine, PSM
explanation:
Solution
• Palliative care is comprehensive care that aims to improve the quality of life for patients and their families facing the problems associated with serious Illness, regardless of prognosis. It can be provided alongside curative treatments.
• Hospice care is a form of palliative care specifically designed for patients with a terminal Illness and a prognosis of six months or less to live. It focuses on providing comfort and support to patients and their families in the final stages of life. often foregoing curative treatments.

Other Options:
Option A: This option incorrectly suggests that palliative care is limited to hospitals and hospice care exclusively occurs in specialized facilities. In reality, both palliative and hospice care can be provided in various settings, including hospitals, homes, hospice facilities, and long-term care facilities.
Option C: This option incorrectly suggests that palliative care is limited to end-of-life care, while in fact, palliative care can be provided at any stage of a serious illness, regardless of prognosis, and can be given alongside curative treatments.
Option D: This option incorrectly states that palliative care emphasizes aggressive treatment to prolong life. In reality, palliative care focuses on providing comfort and improving quality of life, which may or may not include aggressive treatments depending on the patient's goals and prognosis.

Option B: Fracture of femur: A fracture of the femur (thigh bone) is a significant injury and can be classified as grievous hurt, as it involves a fracture of a major bone.
Option C: Permanent privation of hearing: Permanent privation of hearing, meaning the permanent loss of hearing, is a serious and irreversible impairment. This would also be considered grievous hurt.

stem: A 12-year-old school going girl came to OPD with her mother with a complaint of abdominal pain and UPT was found to be positive on further investigation, first step by you as a medical officer?
options: Take consent from the patient's mother
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Inform the police:
• This is a medico-legal case falling under the purview of the POCSO (Protection of Children from Sexual Offences) Act, specifically CRPC 39.
• Given that the patient is a minor, it is a legal and ethical obligation to report suspected cases of child abuse or exploitation.
• This ensures compliance with the mandated reporting requirements, emphasizing the significance of safeguarding the rights and well-being of the minor under established legal frameworks.

Other Options:
Option A: Start physical examination:
In a medico-legal case, immediate initiation of the examination is not permissible. The primary responsibility of the attending physician is to promptly notify the police.
Option C: Take consent from the patient's mother:
Given that the patient is above 12 years of age, she possesses the legal capacity to provide consent for her own physical examination. It is not obligatory to seek consent from the mother in this context.
Option D: Preserve the patient's clothes:
• Preserving the patient's clothes is essential, especially in cases involving a minor, as it can be crucial for preserving potential forensic evidence.
• However, it is not the immediate first step, and the priority lies in promptly informing the police, particularly in medico-legal cases. This action should be carried out with sensitivity and compliance with legal protocols.

stem: Cadaveric spasm seen in all except?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Traumatic asphyxia:
• Cadaveric spasm is observed in various violent deaths. Traumatic asphyxia, while involving force, may not always fall under the conventional understanding of a violent death.
• Traumatic asphyxia is a result of Intense physiological stress rather than direct violence inflicted on the individual by an external agent.

Other Options:
Option A: Suicidal gun-shot wound:
• The presence of cadaveric spasm, characterized by the instantaneous rigor of muscles after death, is notably linked to cases involving intense emotional or physical trauma.
• This phenomenon is often observed in scenarios such as suicidal gunshot wounds, where the individual experiences a violent and traumatic event leading to their demise.
Option B: Electrocution: In cases of electrocution, where there is a sudden and severe electrical shock, the body may exhibit cadaveric spasm due to the abrupt nature of the event.
Option C: Drowning: Drowning involves a struggle for survival, leading to increased stress and physical exertion, which may trigger cadaveric spasms.

stem: A 28 year old patient presented ER with altered sensorium, hypotension, and constricted pinpoint pupil. Which of the following is responsible for poisoning?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Opioid poisoning:
Mechanism: Opioids, such as heroin or morphine, exert their effects by binding to opioid receptors in the central nervous system.
This binding results in central nervous system depression, leading to altered sensorium, respiratory depression, and pinpoint pupils.
Clinical Features:
• Altered mental status
• Respiratory depression
• Constricted pupils (miosis)
• Hypotension
Treatment:
• Opioid toxicity is managed with the administration of naloxone, an opioid receptor antagonist, which can reverse the effects of opioid overdose.

Other Options:
Option B: Datura
Datura contains anticholinergic alkaloids, leading to anticholinergic toxicity. The characteristic features Include altered mental status, dilated pupils (mydriasis), dry skin, and hallucinations. However, the described symptoms of constricted pinpoint pupils are not typical of Datura poisoning.
Treatment: Supportive care, including symptomatic treatment and administration of physostigmine (an anticholinesterase inhibitor).
Option C: Cocaine
Cocaine is a stimulant that Increases sympathetic activity. Clinical features of cocaine toxicity include agitation, hypertension, dilated pupils, and hyperthermia. Constricted pinpoint pupils are not consistent with cocaine toxicity.
Treatment: Benzodiazepines for sedation, antihypertensive agents, and cooling measures in cases of hyperthermia
Option D: Amphetamine
Similar to cocaine, amphetamines stimulate the release of neurotransmitters, leading to increased sympathetic activity.
• The clinical presentation Includes agitation, hypertension, dilated pupils, and hyperthermia. Constricted pinpoint pupils are not characteristic of amphetamine toxicity.
Treatment: Supportive care, including sedation with benzodiazepines and management of complications.

stem: A 33 year old male had a road accident while going back home from the office. Which injury is not seen in a person brought to hospital with a history of RTA?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Chop wound:
A chop wound typically involves a clean, sharp instrument like an axe or machete. In the context of a road traffic accident (RTA), especially involving vehicles, Injuries caused by chopping weapons are uncommon.
RTAs typically result in injuries associated with blunt force trauma, sharp objects from the surroundings, or the vehicle itself.
Other Options:
Option A: Contusion:
Contusion, commonly referred to as a bruise, is characterized by injury to the dermis, where dermal blood vessels rupture, leading to extravasation of blood and the formation of a raised area or swelling.
This occurs due to the force caused by blunt objects such as impacts during a road traffic accident. The margins of a contusion are irregular, contributing to its distinctive appearance.


Option B: Abrasions
Abrasions, or scrapes, occur when the skin rubs against a rough surface.
They are frequently observed in RTAs, especially when individuals come into contact with the road or other abrasive surfaces.


Option C: Laceration
Lacerations are cuts or tears in the skin that can result from sharp objects or edges during an accident.
They are commonly seen in RTAs, especially when there are broken glass or sharp metal components involved.

stem: All are used in identification of dead body except:
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Muscle tissue is not commonly used for Identification due to its rapid postmortem decomposition.
• It lacks unique Individual characteristics that can rellably establish identity.
• Compared to other tissues, muscle is less resistant to environmental degradation.
• It is rarely used for DNA profiling unless no other better-preserved tissue is available.
• Hence, it is not a preferred or standard method for identification of a dead body.

Other Options:
Option A: Teeth
• Teeth are highly resistant to decomposition, fire, and trauma.
• They provide dental records comparison, which is a reliable identification method..
• Forensic odontology is widely used in mass disasters and unidentified bodies.
Option B: Blood
• Blood Is useful for DNA profiling, which is highly specific for identification.
• It can help determine blood group, genetic markers, and individual identity.
• Even small samples can be used with modem forensic techniques.
Option C: Nail
• Nails are resistant to decomposition and can preserve DNA material.
• They can be used for DNA analysis and sometimes contain trace evidence.
• Nail samples are particularly useful when soft tissues are degraded.
