subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
•Datura:
Datura, commonly known as Jimson Weed, Induces poisoning through its tropane alkalolds, primarily impacting the Central Nervous System (CNS)
Symptoms include dryness of skin and mouth, difficulty in swallowing, dilated pupils as an early sign, a staggering gait resembling drunkenness, and slurred speech. Muttering delirium and drowsiness may also manifest, reflecting the profound CNS effects.



•Nicotine:
Nicotine stimulates the cardiovascular system, causing an increase in heart rate and blood pressure.
This effect is attributed to the release of adrenaline and noradrenaline, contributing to its addictive nature and potential cardiovascular risks associated with tobacco use.

•Nitric acid:
• Nitric acid is a corrosive substance that can cause severe skin damage upon contact, leading to burns and tissue destruction.
The acid's caustic nature results in chemical burns, inflammation, and potential scarring, emphasizing the importance of prompt and thorough first aid measures, such as washing the affected area with water.

•Nux Vomica (Strychnine):
Strychnine, found in Nux Vomica, acts on the spinal cord. It blocks inhibitory neurotransmitters, leading to uncontrolled muscle contractions and spasms, primarily affecting the spinal cord.

stem: Among the listed options, which substance is not utilized as a chelating agent in cases of heavy metal poisoning?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Oximes are not typically considered chelating agents in the same way that certain compounds, such as EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) or ligands with multiple donor atoms, are. Chelating agents are molecules that can form multiple bonds to a metal ion, creating a ring-like structure known as a chelate.


Other Options:
Option A: Deferlprone: It is a chelating agent in iron poisoning.
Option B: EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid): EDTA is a synthetic amino acid that forms stable complexes with a variety of metal ions. It is commonly used as a chelating agent in cases of lead poisoning. The EDTA binds to the lead lons in the bloodstream, forming a complex that can be excreted by the kidneys.
Option C: Penicillamine: Penicillamine is a chelating agent used in the treatment of Wilson's disease. It is also used to reduce cystine excretion in cystinuria and to treat patients with severe, active rheumatoid arthritis unresponsive to conventional therapy. Penicillamine is used as a form of immunosuppression to treat rheumatoid arthritis.

stem: Autopsy was conducted on a male with a history of drowning, however on autopsy no water was found in his lungs, and he was labeled a case of dry drowning. What is the cause of death?
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
Laryngospasm:
• In dry drowning or atypical drowning, the most common cause of death is laryngospasm.
• It occurs due to extreme fear leading to excessive catecholamine release.
• There is no water in the alrway and hence there is no froth formation.
• No water is found in the lungs in two conditions:
1. Dry drowning
2. Hydrocution

Other Options:
Option A: Asphyxia: This option is incorrect as laryngospasm is the most common cause of death in dry drowning.
Option B: Vagal Inhibition: This option is Incorrect as in vagal inhibition water is present in the lungs.
Option D: Ventricular fibrillation: This option is incorrect as ventricular fibrillation is not related to the condition.

stem: A 20-year-old boxer is having vision problems and wanted to improve his vision. Which of the following surgeries is contraindicated?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
RK:
• It stands for radial keratotomy. We leave the central comeal zone and we give cuts on the peripheral cornea (4 to 8 cuts given) by diamond knife.
• It is used for myopla correction. As cuts are involved, it is contraindicated for a boxer.

Other Options:
Option B: PRK: This is not contraindicated. Photorefractive keratectomy - We debride the epithelial layer and apply a laser to correct corneal curvature.
Option C: LASIK: This is not contraindicated. We raise a flap here and a laser is used and put the flap back. Hence, curvature is corrected.
Option D: SMILE: This is not contraindicated. A flap is not raised here. Femto laser is used, which focuses on the stroma, and it is cut and we take out the lenticule.

stem: A 55 year old diabetic woman visits a clinic with complaints of blurred vision. On the fundus examination, very severe NPDR was. diagnosed. Which of the following is not seen in very severe NPDR?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Neovascularization of disc:
• NPDR stands for nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy.
• In very severe NPDR, there is no neovascularization of the disc seen.

Other Options:
Option A: Venous beading: Venous beading is present in very severe NPDR.
Option C: Retinal hemorrhage: Retinal hemorrhage is present in very severe NPDR.
Option D: Microaneurysm: Microaneurysms are present in very severe NPDR.

stem: The following instrument is used to measure?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
Proptosis:
• The given instrument is an exophthalmometer and it is used to measure the degree of proptosis in adults.
• We measure the distance from the lateral orbital margin to the apex of the cornea. If it is more than 21 mm or a difference of 2 mm from the other eye, it is called proptosis.
• In children, we use Leudde's exophthalmometer.

Other Options:
Option B: Squint: It is tested by the prism cover test and not by an exophthalmometer.
Option C: Ptosis: It is tested by the scale test and not by an exophthalmometer.
Option D: Diplopla: The given instrument cannot be used to assess diplopia.

stem: A 76-year-old male, admitted for sudden visual loss in the left eye, associated with ocular pain, tearing, and photophobia. What does the following examination shows?
options: Cobalt blue - Tryptan blue - Corneal ulcer
Cobalt green - Rose bengal stain - Corneal ulcer
Cobalt blue - Fluorescein stain - Corneal ulcer
Cobalt blue - Lissamine green - Corneal ulcer
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
Epithelial defects are stained by Fluorescein stain. Fluorescein stain can be best seen under blue light under a slit lamp.

Other Options:
Option A: Cobalt blue - Tryptan blue - corneal ulcer: This combination is incorrect.
Option B: Cobalt green - rose bengal stain - corneal ulcer: This option is incorrect as these dyes do not stain dry tissue.
Option D: Cobalt blue - Lissamine green - corneal ulcer: This option is incorrect as the stain used is Fluorescein stain.

stem: A patient was prescribed with -1D spherical and -2D cylindrical at 180 degrees. What type of refractive error does this patient have?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
With the rule astigmatism:
• The spectacle prescription should first be converted to a retinoscopic reading. The negative sign in the spherical component Indicates myopla. If we solve the prescription, the values on the retinoscope will be -1 on the horizontal axis and -3 on the vertical axis because, in cylindrical lenses, the axis and power of the lens are perpendicular to each other.
• So, the patient has with-the-rule astigmatism as the vertical axis (3D) is more curved than the horizontal.

Other Options:
Options B, C, and D: are incorrect. Refer to Option A for an explanation.

stem: Which of the following is least associated with Iritis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: SLE is the disease least commonly associated with iritis. Rheumatold arthritis has the 2nd least chance of showing iritis in systemic diseases after SLE.

Other Options:
Option B: Behcet's Disease: It is not associated with arthritis but has HLA B5 positive. Patients present with a triad of oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and uveitis (which will further involve iritis).
Option C: Psoriatic Arthropathy: This is incorrect as patients with psoriatic arthropathy will have iridocyclitis.
Option D: Ulcerative colitis: This is incorrect as patients with ulcerative colitis will have iridocyclitis.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with upper respiratory tract infection, orbital pain and presence of pain with eye movement. What is the likely diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The given CT is confirmatory of Orbital cellulitis as there is proptosis visible on the CT and the patient is also having orbital paln.
Other Options:
Option B: Sinusitis: This option is incorrect as proptosis is visible on imaging.
Option C: Both A and B: This option is also incorrect.
Option D: Periosteal abscess: This option is incorrect as CT findings of periosteal abscess are given below - The abscess is seen behind the orbit.

stem: A 23 year old female patient presents to the OPD with the complaints of vertigo which is sudden in onset, sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL), and tinnitus which lasts minutes to hours with congestion in the ear, accompanied nausea, vomiting and vagal symptoms. On Investigations, there is endolymphatic hydrops present. What is the diagnosis of the patient?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
The patient complains of vertigo which is sudden in onset and associated with sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and vagal symptoms. There is also endolymphatic hydrops present. Audiogram reveals an up-sloping graph suggesting Meniere's disease.

Other Options:
Option B: Presbycusis:
This option is incorrect as the audiogram is suggestive of Meniere's Disease.
Option C: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo:
This option is incorrect as the audiogram is suggestive of Meniere's Disease.
Option D: Acoustic neuroma:
This option is incorrect as the audiogram is suggestive of Meniere's Disease. Acoustic neuromas are noncancerous, usually slow-growing tumors that form along the branches of the eighth cranial nerve (also called the vestibulocochlear nerve).

stem: A 37 year old male patient presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral hearing loss with worsen right ear hearing loss. On examination, the doctor diagnoses Otosclerosis and recommends stapedectomy. What would be the findings of tuning fork tests based on which doctor recommends stapedectomy?
options: Renne's Test - positive; Weber's Test - lateralized to left ear
Renne's Test - positive; Weber's Test - lateralized to right ear
Renne's Test - negative; Weber's Test - lateralized to left ear
Renne's Test - negative; Weber's Test - lateralized to right ear
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
Renne's Test - negative; Weber's Test lateralized to right ear. The patient is experiencing bilateral hearing loss, worse in the right ear due to bilateral otosclerosis. in patients with otosclerosis, there is a conductive type of hearing loss hence Rinne will be negative. Weber will be lateralized to the diseased side meaning towards the right ear.

Other Options:
Option A: - Rinne's Test positive; Weber's Test - lateralized to left ear:
In otosclerosis there is a conductive type of hearing loss hence Rinne will be negative.
Option B: - Rinne's Test positive; Weber's Test - lateralized to right ear:
In otosclerosis there is a conductive type of hearing loss hence Rinne will be negative.
Option C: - Rinne's Test - negative; Weber's Test - lateralized to left ear:
Weber will be lateralized to the diseased side meaning towards the right ear.

stem: A 37 year old patient presents to the OPD with the complaint of hearing loss for one year and can understand only shouted or amplified speech. What will be the degree of Impairment according to the WHO classification of ability to understand speech?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
Severe hearing loss:
As the patient understands only shouted or amplified speech, the degree of impairment according to the WHO classification of ability to understand speech would be severe.
• Normal conversation is up to 60dB
• Shouting would indicate that the patient can hear only >60dB; this falls under severe impairment.

Other Options:
Option A: - Mild hearing loss: This option is incorrect as mild hearing loss is at 26 to 40dB only.
Option B: - Moderate hearing loss: This option is incorrect as Moderate hearing loss is at 41 to 60 dB.
Option D: - Profound hearing loss: This option is incorrect as Profound hearing loss is above 80 dB.
Learning objective
Mild - 26 to 40dB
Moderate - 41 to 60 dB
Severe 61 to 80 dB
Profound - >80 dB

stem: A 20 year old patient presents to the OPD with a mass on the right side of nose for 3 months, complaints of bleeding from nose, nasal obstruction, and difficulty in breathing. He gave the history of bathing in the local pond. On examination, red polypoidal mass with whitish dots on the surface is observed. Based on the history, examination and given histopathological image, what will be the diagnosis of the patient?
subject: Pathology, ENT
explanation:
Solution
Rhinosporidiosis: The given histopathology and clinical scenario is suggestive of Rhinosporidiosis, as on histopathology there are sporangia with spores. And also there is bleeding and nasal obstruction.


Other Options:
Option B: Basal Cell Carcinoma:
This option is incorrect as basal cell carcinoma exhibits the findings given below.




Option C: Rhinoscleroma:
This option is incorrect as Rhinoscleroma presents with the following findings.


Option D: Lupus Vulgaris:
This option is incorrect as Lupus vulgaris (also known as tuberculosis luposa) are painful cutaneous tuberculosis skin lesions with nodular appearance, most often on the face around the nose, eyelids, lips, cheeks, ears, and neck.

stem:
Mark the correct statement(s) statements regarding Inner and Outer hair cells?
I. Inner hair cells are in a single row and transmit auditory impulses.
II. Outer hair cells are in 3-4 rows and modulate function of inner hair cells.
III. Outer hair cells are more responsible for movement of Tectorial membrane.
IV. Outer and inner hair cells present in the ratio of 3:1.
options: I, II and IV are correct
subject: ENT, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Inner hair cells are in a single row and transmit auditory impulses - Is correct as type 1 hair cells (Inner hair cells) are single and are different and transmit excitatory impulses.
Outer hair cells are in 3-4 rows and modulate the function of inner hair cells. It is a correct statement as Type 2 also known as outer hair cells are multiple and efferent.
Outer hair cells are more responsible for the movement of the Tectorial membrane - It is incorrect as there is no such rule about the movement of tectorial plates.
Outer and Inner hair cells present in the ratio of 3:1- It is correct as outer hair cells are around 12000 and Inner hair cells are about 3500.

Other Options:
Option B: I, II, and III: This is Incorrect as the 3rd statement is false.
Option C: All are correct: It is incorrect as the 3rd statement is false.
Option D: II, IV: It is incorrect as the 1st statement is also correct.

stem: A neonate is brought to the pediatric ER with respiratory distress, cyanosis, decreased breath sounds on the affected side and scaphoid abdomen. Which of the following should not be performed in this patient?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Bag and Mask Ventilation:
• Bag and mask ventilation is a technique used to manually assist ventilation by delivering positive pressure breaths using a self-expanding bag.
• A neonate with acute respiratory distress with a scaphoid abdomen could potentially be experiencing complications of a diaphragmatic hernia.
• Performing bag and mask ventilation in a child with a diaphragmatic hernia can result in further distension of the stomach and intestines, leading to Increased pressure in the thoracic cavity and potentially worsening respiratory distress.

Other Options:
Options A, C, and D: are Incorrect.
• PPV (Positive Pressure Ventilation): This can be performed but should be done carefully to avoid overinflating the stomach, which can worsen respiratory distress. Intubation with PPV is preferred.
• Oxygen therapy: This is safe and can help improve oxygenation but is usually insufficient alone for severe respiratory distress in CDH.
• Intubation: This is the preferred method to secure the airway and provide effective ventilation while minimizing the risk of inflating the stomach.

stem: A 55-year-old male patient is undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy. During the surgery, a sudden decrease in end-tidal CO2 to 7 mm Hg was observed. The patient's SpO2 was 90% and peak airway pressure was 16 mm Hg. He became hypotensive. Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Anaesthesia, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The most probable diagnosis in this scenario is CO2 embolism. The sudden decrease in end-tidal carbon dioxide (EtCO2), along with a drop in oxygen saturation (SpO2), low blood pressure, and Increased peak alrway pressure, is indicative of a gas embolism. In laparoscopic procedures, CO2 is commonly used to insufflate the abdomen, and an unintentional entry of CO2 into the vascular system can lead to a gas embolism. This condition can cause cardiovascular collapse, hypoxia, and Increased alrway pressure.

Other Options:
Option A: - Pneumothorax:
While pneumothorax can cause decreased oxygen saturation (SpO2) and Increased peak airway pressure, it is less likely in the context of laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Option C: - Endobronchial Intubation:
While it can cause hypoxia, It is less likely to result in the sudden decrease in EtCO2 and the other cardiovascular manifestations described in the scenario.
Option D: - Bronchospasm:
Bronchospasm can raise airway pressure and lower oxygen saturation, but it's less likely to cause the sudden drop in EtCO2 and cardiovascular collapse seen in this case.

stem: Which of the following corresponds to ASA grade III?
options: Patient having systemic illness which is under control or without any functional limitations.
Patient having systemic Illness which is not under control or with functional limitations.
Patient having systemic illness which is a constant threat to life.
subject: Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
Patient having systemic Illness which is not under control or with functional limitations:
ASA classification:
ASA 1: Free from systemic illness
ASA 2: Patient having systemic illness which is under control or without any functional limitations
ASA 3: Patient having systemic Illness which is not under control or with functional limitations
ASA 4: Patient having systemic illness which is a constant threat to life
ASA 5: Moribund patient
ASA 6: Brain dead patient

Other Options:
Options A, B, and D: are Incorrect. Refer to the explanation of the correct answer.

stem: A 7 year old child was being delivered oxygen by nasal cannula at 3L/min. What is the percentage of FIO2 being delivered?
subject: Anaesthesia, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
For each liter per minute (L/rmin) of oxygen flow, you can estimate an increase in FiO2 of about 4%. 1 L/min of O2 corresponds to an FIO2 of 24%
1L/min - 24%
2 L/min - 28%
3 L/min - 32%
4 L/min - 36%
5 L/min - 40%
6 L/min - 44%
In this case, with an oxygen flow rate of 3 L/min, the FIO2 delivered is approximately 32%

Other Options:
Option A: - 24%: An FIO2 of 24% corresponds to 1L/min of oxygen.
Option C: - 44%: An FIO2 of 44% corresponds to 6 L/min of oxygen.
Option D: - 40%: An FIO2 of 40% corresponds to 5 L/min of oxygen.

stem:
Identify the steps of intubation sequence:
a. Flexion of lower cervical spine and extension of Atlanto-occipital joint
b. Inflate cuff of endotracheal tube
c. Introduce a laryngoscope from the right side of mouth
d. Confirm the position of tube
subject: Anaesthesia
explanation:
Solution
Steps of Intubation:
• Flexion of the lower cervical spine and extension of the atlanto-occipital joint It is an optimal and ideal position for Intubation; it is also called the Morning sniffing position.
If you are a right-handed person then you will hold the laryngoscope in your left hand and start entering from the right side of your mouth till you see the epiglottis, once the epiglottis is visible, push the epiglottis anteriorly in adults then vocal cords will be visible.
• After this place the endotracheal tube, then inflate it.
• Cuff pressure should not exceed 25.

Other Options:
Option A: a, b, c, d: This option is Incorrect.
Option B: a, b, d, c: This option is Incorrect.
Option C: b, a, c, d: This option is Incorrect.

stem: A 27-year-old male patient presents with an asymptomatic collarette scale as shown in the image below, Identify the pattern:
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
The image depicts an asymptomatic Collarette scale.
• This presentation is characteristic of Pityriasis rosea, a common skin condition that often begins with a single, large, scaly plaque known as the "herald patch."
• The subsequent rash that follows often has a distinctive "Christmas tree pattern" on the trunk, which corresponds to the orientation of the lesions.

Other Options:
Option B:
• Wickham's striae is a term used to describe the fine white lines or dots that are characteristic of Lichen planus, a chronic inflammatory condition.
• However, Lichen planus typically presents with polygonal, flat-topped papules rather than the described Collarette scale.
Option C: Lichen planus is characterized by Wickham's striae, as mentioned above. However, the description of a Collarette scale is more indicative of Pityriasis rosea.
Option D:
• A Woronoff ring refers to a ring-shaped area of clearing or central healing within a skin lesion.
• It is not specifically associated with Seborrheic dermatitis.
Seborrheic dermatitis typically presents with erythematous, scaly plaques in areas rich in sebaceous glands, such as the scalp and face.

stem:
Which of the following conditions causes non-scarring alopecia?
1. Alopecia areata
2. Telogen effluvium
3. Androgenic alopecia
4. Folliculitis Decalvans
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
Alopecia areata:
Alopecia areata is an autolmmune condition that can cause non-scarring alopecia.
• It typically presents as well-defined, round or oval patches of hair loss on the scalp or other areas of the body.
• While it can lead to non-scarring alopecia, in some cases, it may progress to involve the entire scalp or body, resulting in complete hair loss..

Telogen effluvium:
• Telogen effluvium is a form of non-scarring alopecia characterized by diffuse hair shedding.
• It occurs when a large number of hair follicies enter the telogen (resting) phase simultaneously, leading to increased hair loss.
It is often triggered by various factors such as childbirth, surgery, illness, or significant stress.

Androgenic alopecia:
• Androgenic alopecia, also known as male-pattern baldness or female-pattern baldness, is a genetic condition that leads to progressive hair thinning.
It is a common cause of non-scarring alopecia, especially in a specific pattern on the scalp.

Other Options:
Option D:
• Folliculitis Decalvans is a type of scarring alopecia. Folliculitis decalvans typically presents as clusters of small, red, Inflamed papules or pustules at the site of hair follicles, often accompanied by crusting and scaling.
Over time, the affected area may progress to form patches of smooth, bald skin with visible scarring.

stem: A 64-year-old diabetic patient presents with a lesion on nose which is growing slowly and is pigmented as shown in the image below. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
BCC Is the correct diagnosis based on the provided features.
BCC Is the most common type of skin cancer, often presenting as a slow-growing lesion with nodulo-ulcerative features, telangiectasia (dilated blood vessels), and a tendency to bleed easily.
It is locally Invasive but has a low metastatic potential
Other Options:
Option A:
Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) presents as scaly or crusty lesions, often with elevated, firm nodules on the skin.
Advanced cases may exhibit central ulceration, tenderness, bleeding, and rapid growth. Commonly found in sun-exposed areas.


Option C:
Melanoma is a type of skin cancer that arises from melanocytes. Unlike BCC, melanomas often present as pigmented lesions with Irregular borders and asymmetry.
The provided characteristics, such as noduto-ulcerative features and bleeding to touch, are not typical of melanoma.


Option D:
Discold Lupus Erythematosus (DLE) manifests as erythematous, scaly plaques with well-defined borders.
These lesions can lead to atrophy and scarring over time, accompanied by post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation

stem: Where is the apocrine gland present?
subject: Anatomy, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
Apocrine glands are a type of sweat gland that is concentrated in certain regions characterized by hair follicles.
These glands are particularly abundant in the axilla, nipple, and anogenital areas.
• Unlike eccrine glands, which are distributed widely across the body, apocrine glands become active during puberty and are involved in the production of a milky, viscous sweat.

Other Options:
Option A: Eccrine sweat glands, not apocrine glands, are predominantly found on the palms and soles. Eccrine glands produce a watery, odorless sweat and are involved in regulating body temperature.
Option B: Apocrine glands are not commonly found on the face. Sebaceous glands, responsible for producing sebum (oil), are more abundant in facial skin.
Option D: Apocrine glands are not a prominent feature of the scalp. Sebaceous glands, providing oil to the hair and skin, are more prevalent in this area.

stem: A 15-year-old patient presents with the velvety papillomatous overgrowth of the epidermis shown in the Image below. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
• Acanthosis Nigricans is a skin condition characterized by velvety hyperpigmentation, often occurring in body folds such as the axilla and armpit.
It is commonly associated with insulin resistance, which can be indicative of underlying metabolic conditions such as obesity or diabetes.

Other Options:
Option B:
Tinea versicolor is a fungal infection caused by Malassezia yeast.
It typically presents as lighter or darker patches on the skin and is not associated with the characteristic velvety appearance seen in acanthosis nigricans.
Option C:
• Seborrheic keratosis is a non-cancerous skin growth that appears in various colors, including black.
• However. It usually lacks the velvety texture and is not commonly found in body folds. It is a benign growth often associated with aging.
Option D:
• Atopic dermatitis, or eczema, is a chronic skin condition characterized by red, itchy, and inflamed skin.
It does not typically manifest as dark, velvety lesions and is not commonly located in the axilla and armpit regions.

stem: A 47-year-old male comes with recurrent blisters and oral ulcers for 4 years. The histology is as shown. Diagnosis is:
subject: Dermatology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Pemphigus vulgaris is an autoimmune blistering disorder characterized by the formation of Intraepidermal blisters and erosions on the skin and mucous membranes.
The histology typically shows suprabasal acantholysis, where keratinocytes detach from each other, leading to blister formation.
Other Options:
Option B:
Dermatitis herpetiformis is associated with cellac disease and presents with intensely Itchy, grouped vesicles and papules. often on extensor surfaces.
It is characterized by granular IgA deposits in the dermal papillae, differentiating it from pemphigus vulgaris.


Option C:
Bullous pemphigold is another autoimmune blistering disorder, but it is characterized by subepidermal blisters.
It typically affects older individuals and presents with tense bullae.
Unlike pemphigus vulgaris, acantholysis is not a feature in bullous pemphigoid.


Option D:
Linear IgA disease is characterized by linear IgA deposits along the basement membrane zone.
It presents with subepidermal blisters and is distinct from pemphigus vulgaris, which involves suprabasal acantholysis.

stem:
Consider the following statements:
1. Statement 1-Contemplation - A person who smokes is thinking of quitting smoking.
2. Statement 2-Precontemplation - A person is considering starting morning walks and fits benefits to the health.
3. Statement 3 - Maintenance phase - A person quit smoking one year back and has not smoked since then.
4. Statement 4-Action phase - A person with obesity says he can't do anything as it runs deep in his family.
subject: Psychiatry, PSM
explanation:
Solution
Statement 1-Contemplation: Contemplation is the stage where a person is aware of the need for change and is seriously considering making a change. In this case, a smoker who is thinking of quitting smoking is in the contemplation stage.
Statement 3 Maintenance phase: In this stage, people have sustained their behavior change for more than 6 months and intend to keep moving forward with the change.

Other Options:
Option B, C and D:
Statement 2 Precontemplation: Precontemplation is the stage where person is not yet considering a change or is unaware of the need for change. In this case, the person considering starting morning walks due to its health benefits is not in the pre-contemplation stage. Instead, they are contemplating a positive change.

Statement 4- Action phase: The action phase is the stage where a person actively takes steps to make a change. In this case, a person with obesity who says they can't do anything as it runs deep in the family is not in the action phase, rather, they seem to be expressing a belief or barrier that may hinder them from taking action. This person can be considered to be in the phase of precontemplation.

stem: A 30 year old patient was started on amitriptyline and was found unconscious after the last dose of the drug. Which of the following does not suggest TCA toxicity?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
TCA toxicity is associated with hyperthermia, not hypothermia. Hyperthermia occurs because of the blockage of thermoregulatory sweating due to the anticholinergic effects of TCAs, disrupting normal thermoregulation.

Other Options:
Option A: TCA toxicity often leads to cardiac toxicity, Including arrhythmias. The Inhibitory effect on sodium channels can result in conduction disturbances and arrhythmias.
Option B: Central nervous system effects, such as confusion, are common in TCA toxicity due to the drug's anticholinergic properties.
Option D: Mydriasis (pupillary dilatation) in TCA toxicity is attributed to the anticholinergic effects of tricyclic antidepressants. TCAS block M3 receptors, leading to the disruption of the normal pupillary constriction mediated by the parasympathetic nervous system.

stem: A 28 year old pregnant female with bipolar disorder with a previous history of 4 episodes and no episode for the past 1 year well controlled with lithium 750 mg, now in the third trimester, came to a psychiatrist for advice. What should be done next in the management of this patient?
subject: Psychiatry, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
• Adjustments to medication during pregnancy should be done considering both the well-being of the mother and the potential risks to the fetus.
• Lithium use should be closely monitored.
Dose reduction may be considered especially as the patient is approaching her expected delivery date. Lithium dose should be adjusted during this time to prevent potential toxicity in the newborn.

Other Options:
Option A: Rapid discontinuation can lead to the recurrence of the patient's manic symptoms. This may lead to a worsening of overall outcomes.
Option C: Not recommended without specific indications that would require closer monitoring to avoid toxicity.
Option D: Valproate is typically avoided during pregnancy due to the risk of teratogenic effects and other potential harms to the fetus.

stem: HEADSS assessment is done for:
subject: Pediatrics, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• The HEADSS assessment is typically conducted for Adolescents.
The HEADSS assessment is a psychosocial interview tool used in healthcare. It covers various domains:
1. H: Home environment
2. E: Education and employment
3. A: Activities and peer-related activities
4. D: Drug and substance use
5. S: Suicidality
6. S: Sexuality

stem: A 25 years old female patient on a mood stabilizer has developed hyperammonemia, hepatitis/raised hepatic enzymes and encephalopathy. Which of the following is likely to be responsible?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Hyperammonemla: Valproate can cause hyperammonemla by inhibiting the urea cycle, leading to Impaired ammonia. detoxification. This can result in elevated levels of ammonia in the blood. Hyperammonemia can manifest as encephalopathy.
• Elevated transaminases: Valproate is known to be associated with hepatotoxicity. It can cause liver damage, leading to elevated liver enzymes and, in severe cases, hepatitis.

Other Options:
Option A: Lithium is primarily associated with renal side effects, such as nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, hypothyroidism, leukocytosis, alopecia, and chronic kidney disease. It is not typically linked to hyperammonemia or hepatitis.
Other options C and D: They are not typically associated with these specific effects either

stem: A 76 year old patient of Alzheimer's disease refused to take oral drugs for the last 2 weeks. Which of the following drugs can be prescribed to this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
• Rivastigmine is a reversible acetylcholinesterase used in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
• Rivastigmine is also available in a transdermal patch formulation.
• The patch is applied to the skin, and it releases the medication slowly over a 24-hour period, providing a consistent and continuous therapeutic effect.

Other Options:
Option A: Memantine is an NMDA antagonist, used in moderate-severe disease.
It works by regulating the activity of glutamate, a neurotransmitter involved in learning and memory.
It is administered orally.
Option B:
• Donepezil is a reversible acetylcholinesterase used in the treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer's disease.
It is typically administered orally.
Option D:
• Galantamine is a reversible acetylcholinesterase used in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
It is typically administered orally.

stem: A 4 year old child presents to the ER with breathlessness and pale appearance. No history of fever, vomiting or diarrhea. On chest X-ray, the following object is seen. Identify:
subject: Pediatrics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
It is important to identify the object as the management is different depending on the object. In the case of a coin in the esophagus, the management is observation/expectant but if it's a button battery It can cause erosion and cause fistula formation.
A patient with button battery ingestion has erosions visible on the x-ray image. On the lateral view, the edges are slant which is called the step-off sign. On AP view, a double halo sign can be seen which helps differentiate a button battery from a coin.
• Endoscopic removal should be done.

Other Options:
Option B: This option is incorrect as a double halo sign on the AP view and a step-off sign on the lateral view are present which Is suggestive of a button battery.
Option C: This option is incorrect as the given x-ray is not suggestive of it.
Option D: This option is incorrect as the given x-ray is not suggestive of it.

stem: A 25 year old male football player jumped off the ground to catch the ball and landed on the proximal tibla while the knee was flexed. An MRI was done to diagnose the condition. Identify the marked ligament:
subject: Orthopaedics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
The given Image Is an MRI of the knee joint and PCL is the marked structure as it is located on the posterior aspect and comes posteriorly from the femur to the tibla.

Other Options:
Option B: This is incorrect as it comes anteriorly from the femur to the tibia.
Option C: This option is incorrect as the patellar tendon is attached to the patella and not located posteriorly.
Option D: This option is incorrect as PCL is the correct answer.

stem: A 50 year old male with a history of hypertension complained of knee pain and back pain from last one month. He also reported constipation. X rays of the knee were unremarkable but lab reports showed hypercalcemia. The on duty doctor suspected parathyroid adenoma. What is the investigation of choice for the diagnosis of a parathyroid adenoma?
subject: Radiology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• The Tc-99-Sestamibi scan is the Investigation of choice for diagnosing parathyroid adenoma.
• This nuclear medicine scan uses Technetlum-99m sestamibi, which is taken up by the hyperactive parathyroid tissue. It helps in localizing the parathyroid adenoma with high sensitivity and specificity.
• It is particularly useful in preoperative localization of the adenoma to guide surgical planning.

Other Options:
Option B:
• Sestamibi Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT) can be used as an adjunct to the Tc-99-Sestamibi scan.
• Combines the functional Imaging of the sestamibi scan with the anatomical detall provided by SPECT, Improving localization accuracy.
• It enhances the precision of localization, especially in cases where the adenoma is difficult to locate.
Option C:
• Ultrasound is a non-invasive, readily available imaging technique used to visualize the parathyroid glands.
• It can identify enlarged parathyroid glands and provide structural details.
• However, it is less effective in detecting ectopic parathyroid tissue and may be less sensitive than the Sestamibi scan.
Option D:
• CT (Computed Tomography) scans provide detailed anatomical images and can help locate parathyroid adenomas, especially if they are ectopic.
• However, CT scans lack the functional imaging capability that the Sestamibi SPECT scan provides.

stem: Which of the following statements about the culture taken in a suspected case of Infective endocarditis is correct?
options: Two samples from peripheral lines 30 min apart
Three samples from peripheral lines 1 hour apart
One sample only from peripheral line
Two peripheral samples, two days apart
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Blood Cultures in a patient suspected of infective endocarditis who has not received antibiotics during the prior 2 weeks, three two-bottle blood culture sets containing the appropriate volume of blood (10 mL per bottle) should be obtained from different venipuncture sites over 1-2 hours.
• If the cultures remain negative after 48-72 h, two or three additional blood culture sets should be obtained, and the laboratory should be consulted for advice regarding optimal culture techniques.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect as 3 samples need to be taken 1 hour apart.
Option C: This option is incorrect as one sample is not sufficient and 3 samples need to be taken 1 hour apart.
Option D: This option is incorrect as there is no specification about the timing of sampling, and it does not meet the standard for immediate detection and treatment monitoring.

stem: A 12-year-old boy's father noticed agonal respiration at 5 AM in sleep. He was rushed to the ER where ECG was performed. What could be the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• Coved ST-segment elevation >2 mm in 1 of V1-V3 followed by a negative T wave is potentially diagnostic of Brugada syndrome.
• Brugada syndrome is more prevalent in male patients of Southeast Asian descent.
• The patient will require an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD).

Other Options:
Option A: The ECG shown in this vignette is not suggestive of this condition.
Option C: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is usually related to physical activity (sports-related).
Option D: Characteristic features of absent p wave and irregular RR Intervals are not seen in this patient's ECG.

stem: Comment on the diagnosis from the ECG shown below?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
In second-degree Infranodal heart block, there is a constant PR interval before and after the non-conducted beat.
Each P wave is associated with a QRS complex until there is a skipped beat, in which case the P wave is not followed by a QRS complex.
Other Options:
Option A: There is no change in PR interval.
Seen In:
• Athletes (High vagal tone)
• Rheumatic fever: Aschoff
• Hemochromatosis
• Sarcoidosis
• Endomyocardial fibroelastosis
Option C: It is due to progressive failure of the AV node or complete fallure of the His-Purkinje system.


Option D: It is a pathological phenomenon in which the atria and ventricles beat independently of each other.

stem: A male patient presents to the emergency department he is dyspneic, a known case of COPD. The arterial blood gas report is as follows: pH 7.28; pCO2, 60 mmHg; and HCO3, 28 meq/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• PH 7.28 is decreased, suggestive of acidosis.
• PCO2=60 mmHg. Since it is elevated, the acidosis is likely respiratory.
• The bicarbonate is 28 meq/L Implying that compensation is partial (partial compensation/Inadequate compensation).

Other Options:
Option B: This option is incorrect. In a patient with fully compensated respiratory acidosis, the pH is expected to be within the normal range, unlike in this scenario.
Option C: This option is incorrect since this patient is currently acidotic.
Option D: This option is incorrect since this patient has respiratory acidosis.

stem: 73-year-old woman with Type 2 DM and hypertension and developed features of heart failure and expired. Post-mortem cardiac blopsy slide is given below:
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The given Image is suggestive of restrictive cardiomyopathy, as there is pink amyloid material present in between the myocytes.
Restrictive Cardiomyopathy:
1. Rarest form of cardiomyopathy
2. STIFF HEART: Normal size ventricles but atria is dilated due to having to contract against stiff ventricles
3. Pink Hyaline deposits (amyloid protein) in the myocardium cause the stiffness of the ventricles.
Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect. The histopathological specimen in patients with this condition shows multinucleated myocytes.


Option C: Myocyte disarray on histopathological examination is a classic feature of Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.


Option D: Histopathological findings in patients with this type of cardiomyopathy is characterized by interstitial infiltrates of lymphocytes, leukocytes and macrophages. Myocardial fibrosis and contraction bands with or without myocyte necrosis is also seen in these specimens.

stem: A 40-year-old patient of grade II steatohepatitis of liver presents with regular intake of 2-3 alcohol drinks daily and has BMI of 29. What will be the most appropriate advice to the patient?
options: Lifestyle modification - vitamin E + Ursodeoxycholic acid
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The likely cause of steatohepatitis in this patient is routine alcohol consumption. The patient is also overweight.
• Diet and Exercise: Lifestyle changes and dietary modifications that result in weight loss and/or improve insulin sensitivity are the primary treatments for NAFLD.
• Many studies indicate that loss of 5-8% of body weight improves steatosis and that greater weight loss (l.e., 7-10%) Improves steatohepatitis and hepatic fibrosis.

Other Options:
Option B: This option is incorrect as this treatment is recommended for patients with nonalcoholic steatohepatitis.
Option C: This option is incorrect as this treatment is recommended for nonalcoholic steatohepatitis.
Option D: Appropriate lifestyle modifications with routine exercise would be the most suitable recommendation for this patient.

stem: Stroke patient presents with inability to identify objects but can identify the color, texture, shape of objects. Which of the following lobes is involved?
subject: Medicine, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• Patient history is suggestive of visual agnosia.
• A patient with visual agnosia is unable to either name a visually presented object or describe its use.
• Face and object recognition disorders also can result from simultanagnosla as seen in patients with Balint's syndrome, in which case they are known as apperceptive agnosias as opposed to the associative agnosias that result from inferior temporal lobe lesions.

Other Options:
Option A: Lesions in a patient's frontal lobe can lead to certain symptoms such as trouble with learning, deficits in visual-motor function, executive dysfunction and problems with attention (planning, focusing, and inhibition).
Option B: Parietal Lobe, Right Damage to this area can cause visuospatial deficits (e.g., the patient may have difficulty finding their way around new, or even familiar, places). Parietal Lobe, Left - Damage to this area may disrupt a person's ability to understand spoken and/or written language.
Option D: Unilateral occipital lobe lesion causes contralateral homonymous hemianopla. It is a visual field defect on the same side of both eyes contralateral to the site of the lesion. Lesions of the occipital lobe due to the posterior cerebral artery infarct cause homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing.

stem: Comment on the lung disease:
subject: Medicine, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
•The given flow volume loop represents an obstructive lung disease, and among the given options, bronchiectasis is the only obstructive lung disease.
•Obstructive lung diseases are characterized by obstruction or narrowing of the airways, which makes it difficult for air to flow out of the lungs during exhalation.
•This results in diminished expiratory flows, meaning that it takes longer for the lungs to empty than normal. (The Expiratory curve shows a scooped-out appearance)
• Additionally, due to the air trapping caused by the obstruction, lung volumes, particularly the residual volume (RV) and total lung capacity (TLC), Increase, resulting in the shift of the curve to the left (larger volumes).

Other Options:
Options A, B and C: are restrictive lung diseases, and the flow volume shifts to the right in restrictive lung diseases.
In restrictive lung diseases, the lungs have decreased compliance, which limits their ability to expand fully during inhalation. As a result, lung volumes (decreased vital capacity) are smaller during each respiratory cycle.

stem: A diabetic male with chest pain and palpitations came to the ER. ECG was taken, which showed NSTEMI. Treatment:
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Since the pathology in NSTEMI is a platelet-raised thrombus, the patient is treated with dual antiplatelet therapy along with low molecular weight heparin.
• LMWH is preferred over unfractionated heparin since it does not require monitoring of partial thromboplastin time and is easier to administer.
• Thrombolysis is not indicated in NSTEMI.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect.
Option B: This option is incorrect since dual antiplatelet therapy along with LMWH Is the initial treatment of choice.
Option D: This option is Incorrect.

stem: Most common cause of hypercalcemia in a hospitalized patient is:
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• In malignancy-associated hypercalcemia, the disease is usually not occult; rather, symptoms of malignancy bring the patient to the physician, and hypercalcemia is discovered during the evaluation.
• In such patients, the interval between detection of hypercalcemia and death, especially without vigorous treatment, is often 6 months.
• Example: Squamous cell carcinoma of the lungs.

Other Options:
Option B: This option is incorrect as hypercalcemia in an adult who is asymptomatic is usually due to primary hyperparathyroidism.
Option C: This option is incorrect as Hypercalcemia in an adult who is asymptomatic is usually due to primary hyperparathyroidism.
Option D: Hyperparathyroidism: This option is incorrect as it is latrogenic.

stem: A 47 yr old woman presents with bilateral symmetrical tender joints of hands, and ankles. She complains of early morning stiffness.. On examination, no erythema or redness was found. What could be the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
The given clinical picture is suggestive of a diagnosis of Rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disease characterized by bilateral symmetrical tender joints along with early morning stiffness.

Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect as Osteoarthritis typically involves the knee joint and patients are usually between 50-60 years of age.
Option B: Psoriatic arthritis is an autoimmune disorder with a similar presentation. This condition usually involves fewer joints and the distal interphalangeal joints are spared. Patients also present skin findings of psoriasis.
Option C: Gout is a disease caused due to high levels of uric acid in the blood. Men are more commonly affected. The Involved joints are usually tender and erythematous.

stem: Which of the following is not a component of metabolic syndrome?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• Diagnostic features of metabolic syndrome:
• Abdominal circumference:
1. Globally males > 102 cm
2. Globally females > 88 cm
3. Indian male > 90 cm
4. Indian females > 80 cm
• Impaired glucose tolerance test/Insulin resistance:
1. FBS = 100-125 mg/dl
2. 2 hour postprandial = 140-199 mg/dl
3. The patient already on oral hypoglycemic drugs
• Increase in peripheral resistance, hypertension > 130/85 mmHg
• Increased triglycerides > 150 mg%
• HDL decreased (below 40-50 mg%)

Other Options:
Options B, C and D: are Incorrect. Refer to the explanation of the correct answer.

stem: A young patient developed hyperpigmentation of distal fingers after being hospitalized post adrenalectomy. What could be the possible cause?
subject: Medicine, Endocrinology
explanation:
Solution
• Nelson syndrome is a rare condition that occurs in patients who have had both adrenal glands removed (bilateral adrenalectomy) as a treatment for Cushing's disease.
• It is characterized by the development of a pituitary adenoma that produces excess adrenocorticotropin hormone (ACTH), which leads to hyperpigmentation of the skin, especially in areas exposed to friction and pressure, such as the distal fingers.
• In patients with Cushing's disease, Increased levels of ACTH lead to overproduction of cortisol. Considering the patient has undergone bilateral adrenalectomy, there is an absence of cortisol response to elevated ACTH.
• Due to the absent production of cortisol, increased levels of CRH (Corticotropin-releasing hormone) are produced and released from the hypothalamus which in turn activates the pituitary to increases prooplomelanocortin production.
• Proopiomelanocortin is a common precursor of MSH (Melanocyte stimulating hormone) and ACTH leading to hyperpigmentation.

Other Options:
Option A:
• Addison's disease involves primary adrenal insufficiency, where the adrenal glands do not produce sufficient steroid hormones.
• Hyperpigmentation can occur due to increased ACTH levels, but Addison's disease is typically associated with adrenal atrophy or autoimmune destruction, not post-adrenalectomy.
Option B:
• Conn's syndrome, or primary hyperaldosteronism, is characterized by excessive production of aldosterone by the adrenal glands.
• It does not typically cause hyperpigmentation. Symptoms are usually related to hypertension and hypokalemia.
Option D:
• Cushing syndrome is caused by prolonged exposure to high levels of cortisol. It usually leads to symptoms such as central obesity, moon face, and purple striae, rather than hyperpigmentation of the fingers.
• Hyperpigmentation can occur if ACTH levels are high, but this is more typical of ectopic ACTH production rather than the syndrome itself.

stem: A 35-year-old farmer from Tamil Nadu presented with fever, myalgia, headache and cough. During physical examination a lesion is seen on the chest. Lung auscultation shows extensive bilateral rales. Blood Investigation showed positive IgM for scrub typhus. What would be the appropriate treatment?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Notice the eschar on the chest.
• Drug of choice for scrub typhus is Doxycycline as per page 1430, CMDT 2023.
• However, updated guidelines mention combination of doxycycline with azithromycin due to increasing reports of resistance.

Other Options:
Option A: This option was right according to the previous guideline but in the recent updates treatment of choice for scrub typhus is Doxycycline with azithromycin.
Option B: This option is incorrect.
Option C: This option is Incorrect

stem: A 26-year-old female presents with menorrhagia and excessive bleeding after minor surgery. She gave the history of father and sister having some bleeding issues and the pattern of inheritance was determined to be autosomal dominant. What is the most Ilkely disorder?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
It is a more common cause of bleeding and follows the Autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance.
The given scenario also shows autosomal dominant Inheritance pattern as father and her sister is also having some bleeding problem.

Other Options:
Options B and C: Christmas diseases are X linked recessive and rare to affect.
Option D: Have both autosomal dominant and recessive Inheritance but AD pattern of inheritance and is less common in Factor 10 deficiency.

stem: A 27-year-old female presents with palpitations, weight loss, tremors, sweating, and diarrhea. What is the next test that will help in establishing the diagnosis in this patient?
subject: Medicine, Endocrinology
explanation:
Solution
• These tests are the primary screening tests for thyroid function.
• A low TSH and high free T4 levels would indicate hyperthyroidism, which matches the symptoms described.
• TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone) is produced by the pituitary gland and stimulates the thyroid gland to produce thyroid hormones.
• Free T4 is the active form of thyroxine, the main hormone produced by the thyroid gland.
• Measuring both TSH and free T4 provides a clear picture of thyroid function. TSH levels are very sensitive to changes in thyroid hormone levels, making it the most reliable initial test for thyroid dysfunction.

Other Options:
Option B:
• Anti-TPO antibodies are useful in diagnosing autoimmune thyroid diseases like Hashimoto's thyroiditis or Graves' disease but are not the first-line test for initial diagnosis of hyperthyroidism.
• They do not provide direct information about thyroid hormone levels or immediate thyroid function.
Option C:
• Total T3 can be elevated in hyperthyroidism but is less rellable than free T4 for the initial diagnosis.
• It can be influenced by factors such as binding proteins and does not provide as comprehensive an assessment as TSH and free T4 levels.
Option D:
• Ultrasound of the neck can help in assessing the thyroid gland's size, structure, and presence of nodules but does not provide functional information about thyroid hormone levels.
• It is more useful as a secondary test once thyroid dysfunction is established or to further investigate structural abnormalities.

stem: Patient was taking ampicillin for URTI. Then he started having watery diarrhea for the past 3 days. Drug of choice to manage this condition:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The diagnosis is pseudomembranous colitis. The drug of choice in this patient is fidaxomicin/vancomycin.

Other Options:
Option A: Metronidazole can be used for severe diarrhea associated with C. difficile but it is not the drug of choice. Metronidazole is used only if vancomycin or fidaxomicin is not readily accessible.
Option C: Fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin can potentially cause pseudomembranous colitis.
Option D: It is a monoclonal antibody used in recurrent pseudomembranous colitis. It is not the first-line treatment in this condition.

stem: A 33-year-old patient presents with high-grade fever for the past week. On examination neck stiffness and Kernig's sign was positive. CSF analysis showed lymphocytic pleocytosis - 80 mg/dL glucose - 20 mg/dL while RBS=140 mg/dL. What is your diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
CSF sugar/ Random Blood sugar is noted to be low (40/140) with elevated CSF protein.
This along with CSF lymphocytosis favors a diagnosis of Tubercular meningitis.

Other Options:
Options B and D: Coccidioides meningitis and HSV-1 encephalitis: These are ruled out as CSF sugar is normal in viral meningitis/encephalitis.
Option C: Bacterial meningitis is characterized by the presence of PMN Cells, therefore it can be ruled out.

stem: A 35-year-old HIV positive man presents with neck rigidity and papilledema. CSF sample showed a capsulated organism positive for India Ink staining. Which of the following statements is true?
options: Delay antiretroviral therapy by 4-6 weeks after the initiation of antifungal treatment.
Brain imaging shows changes in 80% of patients.
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Due to the risk of IRIS (Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome), ART should be deferred by 4-6 weeks after the Initiation of antifungal treatment.

Other Options:
Option A: It is unlikely that intracranial pressure is normal since the patient has papilledema on examination.
Option C:
• Fluconazole alone is not used to manage Cryptococcal meningitis.
• Acute/Induction: Amphotericin B (0.7 mg/kg/day) + Flucytosine 25 mg QID for 14 days
• Consolidation: Fluconazole 400 mg/day for 8-10 days until CSF is sterile
• Maintenance: Fluconazole 200 mg/day lifelong
Option D: Brain imaging shows changes in >80% of patients. It is incorrect as brain imaging showing meningoencephalitis is seen in less than 30 percent of cases and classical soap bubble appearance is not commonly seen as well.

stem: A 67-year-old diabetic man presents after a fall. He is having a high fever, cough and breathing difficulty and appears confused. CXR shows lobar consolidation. Labs show elevated BUN and creatinine. Which is the most appropriate management?
options: Discharge home on amoxicillin with clavulanic acid.
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Parameters to be considered for the CURB-65 score in this case are: age, elevated BUN, and presence of confusion.
• The total score is 3 which indicates that the patient needs hospitalization.
Since the patient is also diabetic, he needs to be started on the following:
1. Respiratory Fluoroquinolones
2. Amoxicillin + Clavulanic acid and Azithromycin
3. Cephalosporin + Azithromycin
4. Doxycycline

Other Options:
Option B: This option is incorrect since the patient cannot be treated with a cephalosporin alone.
Option C: This option is incorrect since the patient needs to be treated on an inpatient basis.
Option D: This option is Incorrect since the patient needs to be treated on an inpatient basis.

stem: A known RA patient presented to ER with hypotension. She was on medication for RA treatment but abruptly stopped medications. HR 110 bpm BP is 80/40 mm Hg, blood sugar - 50 mg/dL, Na=130 meq/L and K=6.0 meq/L. What should be the most appropriate management?
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
The clinical presentation is of Addisonian crisis that is managed with hydrocortisone.
Low Blood pressure, low Na+ and increased K+ is due to Low aldosterone.
• Hypoglycemia of patients is due to low cortisol
All this suggests an Addisonian crisis and this will be managed by Hydrocortisone.
• An Addisonian crisis, or adrenal crisis, is a potentially life-threatening condition that results from an acute insufficiency of adrenal hormones (glucocorticoid or mineralocorticoid) and requires immediate treatment.
• This will stabilize Low BP and Hypoglycemia.

Other Options:
Option A: Fast IV fluids
Option C: Dopamine Infusion
Option D: Soda bicarbonate

stem: A child is brought to the ER in an unconscious state after severe vomiting and abdominal pain episodes. HR=100/min and BP=80/60 mm Hg, work up shows pH=7.2, blood glucose 300 mg/dL, ketone 3+. What should be the best management?
options: Infusion of 0.05-0.1 U/kg dose Insulin after 1 hour of IVF
0.1 Unit/kg Insulin stat followed infusion of 0.5-0.1 U/kg dose Insulin and IVF
0.1 U/kg Insulin subcutaneous stat followed by IVF
IVF followed by subcutaneous 0.1 U/kg Insulin
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• The patient lab reports and symptoms are suggestive of diabetic ketoacidosis.
• Patient is vomiting, which implies moderate or greater dehydration without shock.
Start on Normal saline 0.9% to 10 to 20 mL/kg over 20 to 30 min and correct fluid deficit over next 24 to 48 hours.
Patient should also be assessed and treated for hypokalemia.
• Then start continuous IV Insulin Infusion of 0.05-0.1 unit/kg/nour starting 1 hour after initiation of fluids.

Other Options:
Option B: For pediatric patients with DKA, stat dose of insulin is not recommended. Fluid status and potassium should be corrected before initiating insulin.
Option C: Fluid status and potassium should be corrected before initiating insulin.
Option D: IV fluids followed by an insulin Infusion is the most appropriate treatment recommendation.

stem:
Work up of a 25-year-old hotel worker shows the following profile:
• HBsAg - Negative
• Anti-HBsAg - Negative
• HBeAg - Negative
• Anti-HBe - Negative
• Total anti HBc - Positive
What is the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Total Anti-HBc is the most constant marker of exposure (current or post infection).

Other Options:
Option A: Needs presence of surface antigen (HBsAg) and IgG Anti-HBc.
Option B: Has circulating hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) with normal aminotransferase levels, minimal or no histological evidence of liver injury, undetectable HBeAg and level of HBV DNA undetectable or present at threshold <10 IU/ml.
Option D: Needs IgM anti-HBc for diagnosis.

stem: A 42-year-old female with complaints of recurrent episodes of abdominal pain and cramps. Endoscopy is done and diagnosed as UC. She currently has 3-4 episodes of bloody stools per day. Appropriate initial treatment will be:
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The clinical picture of this patient is suggestive of mild ulcerative colitis, and for mild ulcerative colitis - Sulfasalazine is used.

Other Options:
Option A: It is used in cases of exacerbation of ulcerative colitis.
Option C: Azathioprine (AZA) is a medication used in the management and treatment of active rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and the prevention of kidney transplant rejection.
Option D: Bezlotoxumab is indicated to reduce the recurrence of Clostridium difficile Infection (CDI) in patients who are receiving antibacterial drug treatment for CDI and are at high risk for CDI recurrence.

stem: Which of the following anti-diabetic drug is recently approved to be used in patients with heart fallure with low ejection fraction and reduced urine output?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Anti-diabetic drugs reducing cardiovascular mortality are SGLT2 inhibitors (Empagliflozin, Dapagliflozin) and GLP-1 agonists (Semaglutide, Dulaglutide).
Empagliflozin is an example of an SGLT2 inhibitor.

Other Options:
Option A: It is a DPP-4 inhibitor. The DPP 4 enzyme is responsible for the breakdown of incretins such as GLP-1.
Option C: These drugs have been shown to cause dose-dependent fluid retention, hence this drug class is generally avoided in patients with heart fallure.
Option D: Insulin degludec is a long-acting insulin used to treat hyperglycemia caused by type 1 and 2 diabetes mellitus.

stem: Patient was being treated In ICU for sepsis. ABG shows pH=6.9, pO2=80 mm Hg, pCO2=55 mm Hg, HCO3=10 meq/L. Repeat ABG after 2 hours shows pH=6.7, pCO2=20 mm Hg, pO2=100 mm Hg, HCO3=5 meq/L. Which is most plausible to explain the change seen In ABG report?
options: Same or less heparin in ABG sample
Excess air and more heparin in ABG sample
Less air and more heparin in ABG sample
Less air and less heparin in ABG sample
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The ABG values are suggestive of Metabolic acidosis with respiratory acidosis.
• Heparin is a sulfated mucopolysaccharide with acidic properties. An excess of heparin in the ABG sample can influence the following effects:
1. If the pH is normal or alkaline, to begin with - Acidemia increases - pH will fall on contact with the acidic heparin.
2. If the pH is very acidic, to begin with - Acidemia decreases - pH will rise as the mildly acidic heparin reduces the greater acidity of the blood.

Other Options:
Option A: Excessive alr and less heparin In ABG sample: This option is incorrect.
Option C: Less air and more heparin in ABG sample: This option is incorrect.
Option D: Less air and less heparin In ABG sample: This option is Incorrect.

stem: Which of the following part of the inguinal canal is formed by fibres of internal oblique and trans abdominal muscles?
subject: Anatomy, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• Roof of Inguinal canal is formed by fibres of internal oblique and trans abdominal muscles.
• Posterior wall is formed by conjoint tendons and fascia transversalis, the components of conjoint tendon are internal oblique and trans abdominal muscles.

Other Options:
Option A: It is mainly formed by external oblique aponeurosis.
Option C: It is mainly formed by inguinal ligament.

stem: What is the primary prognostic factor that significantly influences the outcome of breast cancer?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Lymph Node status is the most important prognostic factor in all cancers according to TNM staging of cancer.
• In breast cancer, the most important prognostic factor is axillary lymph node status.
• The most important prognostic factor in metastatic breast cancer is ER and PR status.

Other Options:
Option B: This is not a primary prognostic factor that significantly influences the outcome of breast cancer.
Option C: This is not a primary prognostic factor that significantly influences the outcome of breast cancer.
Option D: The most important prognostic factor in metastatic breast cancer is ER and PR status which is also known as hormone receptor status.

stem: Which is not a risk factor for Ca Gall Bladder?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
The presence of a polyp <10mm is not a risk factor for carcinoma of the gall bladder.
• A polyp measuring more than 1cm with adenomatous features is a risk factor for gall bladder carcinoma.

Other Options:
Option A: Calcified gall bladder or gall bladder is one of the most important risk factors for cancer of the gall bladder.
Option B:
• It is a risk factor for gallbladder cancer.
• Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is a chronic liver disease in which the bile ducts inside and outside the liver become Inflamed and scarred, and eventually narrowed or blocked. When this happens, bile builds up in the liver and causes further liver damage.
Option D: The presence of gallstone >3 cm is a risk factor for gallbladder cancer.

stem: A young female presenting with pain and vomiting in the ER. Clinical examination reveals a swelling lateral and below pubic tubercle. X-ray shows intestinal obstruction. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Surgery, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The patient being female is the first clue.
• The neck of the hernia is below and lateral to the pubic tubercle inferential hernia.
So, the likely diagnosis is a femoral hernia since examination reveals a swelling lateral and below the pubic tubercle.
• Femoral ring and canal is very rigid causing a high risk of strangulation.

Other Options:
Option A: Adhesions can lead to obstruction, but there will be no swelling.
Option B: The location in this case will be above and medial to the pubic tubercle, not lateral and below the pubic tubercle.
Option D: Appendicular mass can lead to swelling but it is located in the right iliac fossa, not lateral and below the pubic tubercle.

explanation:
Solution
• Chest 9% + abdomen 9% + right upper limb 9% (anterior part = 4.5% + posterior part = 4.5%) + whole right thigh 9% (anterior part of thigh = 4.5% + posterior part of thigh = 4.5%)
Total = 36%

Other Options:
Options A, B and D: are incorrect. Refer to the explanation of the correct answer.

stem: Classification of bile duct injury taking location and thermal injury into duct and vessel into account:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Stewart Way Classification: This classification of bile duct Injury takes into account the location and thermal injury to the duct and vessel.

Other Options:
Option A: The Bismuth classification is a simple classification based on the location of Injury and does not consider factors such as thermal injury to duct and vessel.
Option B: It is a modified Bismuth classification that allows for differentiation between small and serious injuries sustained during laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Option D: The new classification covers lesions localized above the bifurcation of the common bile duct in more detall, thus categorizing patients based on location but not on thermal injury.

stem: Post Orchiectomy, the specimen shows Reinke's Crystals. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Pathology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Reinke's crystals are seen in the Leydig cells of testis.
Leydig cell tumor is associated with gynecomastia.
• Reinke's crystals are rod-like cytoplasmic inclusions that can be found in Leydig cells.
Painless swelling or mass in relation to the testis.
It is a Sex Cord/Gonadal Stromal Tumor.

Reinke's Crystals are not seen in this type of tumor.
Most common testicular tumor seminoma (3rd and 4th decades).
Beta-hCG is a tumor marker seen in patients with semínoma.
Markers based on the bulk of the disease These are elevated in Seminoma:
1. LDH
2. PLAP
3. CGT
Option C: Reinke's crystals are not seen in metastatic disease.
Option D:
Alpha-fetoprotein is the tumor marker.
Most common testicular tumor in children (1-3 years) - Yolk sac tumor.
Testicular tumor with best prognosis - yolk sac tumor.

stem: A patient underwent cholecystectomy. The histopathology reveals carcinoma with invasion into the muscularis. What is the next best step?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Tla - Involvement of lamina propria.
• T1b - Involvement of muscularis propria.
In the gall bladder, 2 layers are missing, these are the muscularis mucosa and submucosa. There is direct Involvement of muscularis propria after lamina propria.
So Stage here is T1b (Stage-18).
• For Stages I B to III B, the treatment of choice is Extended Cholecystectomy (Where we remove segments IV B and V of liver and lymph nodes).

Other Options:
Option A: It is not done in this case and is of no benefit.
Option B: It is not done in this case and is of no benefit.
Option C: This is done when the margin of the cystic duct is also involved.

stem: Incorrect statement about Choledochal Cyst:
options: The cyst can be opened directly and drained
Todani classification is used for grading
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
This statement is incorrect as the management of Choledochal Cysts varies according to the type and extent of Involvement.
• Type I and Type IV Choledochal Cysts are managed with complete excision of the choledochus and restoration of bile flow by Roux en Y Hepaticojejunostomy or Hepaticoduodenostomy.
• Type II Choledochal Cysts are managed with simple excision of the cyst/diverticulectomy with occlusion of the diverticular neck.
• Type III Choledochal Cysts can be managed with sphincterotomy and a biopsy of the cyst epithelium in most cases.
• Type V Choledochal cysts are managed with hepatic resection/transplant depending on extent of the disease.

Other Options:
Option A: This is true since certain types of choledochal cysts are associated with an increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma.
Option C: The choledochal cyst is classified according to Todani's classification. The appropriate management is then decided.
Option D: In choledochal cyst the is an abnormal pancreaticobiliary duct junction and there is reflux of the pancreatic enzymes. The presence of these pancreatic enzymes in the CBD then causes dilation of the CBD.

stem: Open cardiac massage is what kind of wound?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
• There is a major breakage in sterile technique in open cardiac massage. This is considered category 3.
• Open cardiac massage is typically performed when there is inadequate cardiac output, after emergency thoracotomy for penetrating chest trauma, during pericardial tamponade, or during cardiac arrest following recent chest surgery.
Image showing open cardiac massage


Other Options:
Option A: This option is incorrect as there is spillage in open cardiac massage and a breach in sterile technique.
Option C: Open cardiac massage is not considered a dirty wound.
Option D: Due to the organ involved and extensive spillage, open cardiac massage cannot be classified as clean contaminated.

stem: A female presents with a non-palpable lump in the breast. The Biopsy and IHC reveals luminal A. What is included under Luminal A?
options: ER positive, PR positive, Her 2 neu positive
ER positive, PR positive, Her 2 neu negative with low proliferation
ER positive, PR positive. Her 2 neu negative with high proliferation

explanation:

• Special feature of luminal A is that the cell proliferation activity is lower than other types, that's why it is most common type and have best prognosis.
